Potential Acceleration in the 2H As we progress into 2H 2025, Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM)’s July banking system data continues to point to improving loan momentum. BNM reported that loan growth accelerated to 5.4% YoY in July (June: +5.1% YoY), bringing YTD growth to 2.5%, which is broadly in line with the 2.7% YTD increase recorded in July 2024. The pickup in momentum is driven by stronger business lending, as corporates likely gained clarity on tariff-related uncertainties that had previously weighed on sentiment and investment plans. On the consumer side, loan growth remained supportive at +5.8% YoY. Outlook Supported by a More Sanguine Macro Backdrop Looking ahead, we expect loan momentum to gain further traction for the remaining part of 2025, underpinned by rising applications and approvals, as well as a supportive macro backdrop. Consumer lending (c. 58% of system loans) should benefit from firmer household sentiment, resilient labour market conditions, accommodative monetary policy, and recent fiscal support measures. Business and SME loan growth is also poised to gradually accelerate, aided by greater policy clarity in US tariffs on Malaysian imports. Against this backdrop, we maintain our full-year 2025 loan growth forecast of 5.7%. Mixed Outlook Across Banks Under Coverage Loan growth outlook remains mixed across banks under our coverage, though momentum is clearly stronger among smaller players. In the recent 2QCY25 results, Affin and Alliance continue to deliver above-industry growth, while Hong Leong Bank also stands out among mid-sized peers with gross loans expanding 7.8% YoY, ahead of system growth. Both Alliance and Hong Leong Bank are benefiting from diversification across multiple segments such as SME, commercial, and consumer loans, led by mortgages and auto financing. Affin, meanwhile, demonstrated robust growth in consumer and enterprise banking. Although corporate banking experienced some repayments earlier in the year, management expects stronger traction in the 2H, particularly in working capital and trade financing. Smaller Banks to Outperform Larger Peers We forecast smaller banks to maintain their lead. Alliance remains bullish, with gross loans rising 9.9% YoY in 1QFY26, within its 8–10% full-year target, supported by diversification into rising economic corridors such as Penang and Sarawak, where loan growth reached 13%. Affin is targeting a stronger 12% expansion, underpinned by its enterprise banking focus and growing demand for trade financing, while Hong Leong Bank is guiding for 6–7% growth. Among the larger banks, Public Bank is guiding for a steadier 5–6% loan growth in FY25, underpinned by broad-based domestic lending across commercial property, SME, and hire purchase segments. YTD, growth has been tracking well, with the total loan book expanding at an annualised rate of 5.1%. Larger Banks More Cautious Meanwhile, we note that banks with higher corporate exposure remain less optimistic about their loan outlook. While SME lending has continued to expand, larger corporate loans have largely been flat. RHB Bank and Maybank, in particular, have guided for a more cautious stance. RHB revised its FY25 loan growth target down from 6–7% to 5–6% in 1Q, citing ongoing US– China trade tensions. That said, management remains positive about domestic-driven demand, expecting stronger disbursements in 2H on the back of commercial and middle-market lending. Maybank also trimmed its loan growth guidance to around 3% from 5–6% previously. Groupwide loan growth was modest at 0.8% YoY in the recent quarter, with Malaysia and Singapore performing better (+5% and +4% YoY respectively), but weighed down by Indonesia (-9% YoY) While management expects Malaysia and Singapore to grow above system levels, overall growth will remain capped by the significant contraction in Indonesia. CIMB’s loan growth was similarly muted at +1% YoY, reflecting contractions in Wholesale Banking. Nonetheless, management expects a recovery in 2H as business pipelines gradually convert. AMMB, on the other hand, continues to prioritise portfolio repositioning, focusing on quality over volume. While this approach may weigh on retail and SME lending in the near term, loan growth is expected to broadly align with GDP, supported by mass affluent mortgages and a turnaround in auto finance. FX and NIM Headwinds in Overseas Operations While domestic loans should remain healthy, overseas operations present a mixed picture. Hong Leong Bank reported strong expansion in Singapore and Vietnam, with loans rising 16.8% and 17.7% YoY in local currency, while its associate Bank of Chengdu delivered robust 18% loan growth. However, larger banks with wider overseas footprints, like CIMB and Maybank, continue to face greater exposure to FX translation effects and margin compression. For example, CIMB’s Indonesia and Singapore operations recorded solid constant-currency growth of 6.8% and 9.0% YoY respectively, but group-wide reported loan growth was only 1% due to currency headwinds. Recommendation In summary, we expect the domestic market to remain the primary driver of loan growth for the banks. Consumer-related lending remains another key growth pillar, especially mortgages and auto financing. SME and business lending are also emerging as important drivers. Smaller banks are outperforming larger institutions in loan growth, driven by diversified strategies and robust pipelines, particularly in the retail and SME/enterprise sectors. Conversely, larger banks with significant corporate or overseas exposure show more cautious outlooks. Despite these varying performances, overall system data points to improving loan momentum, with banks expecting stronger disbursements in the second half of the year. This optimism reflects not just healthier business pipelines but also a gradual recovery in confidence and policy clarity, setting the stage for stronger lending momentum, going forward. With that, we maintain 2025 loan growth forecast at 5.7% for now, underpinned by consumer and business loan growth of 6.3% and 4.9%. We also maintain ourOVERWEIGHTstance on the sector, given its resilience in asset quality, capacity to support credit flows and decent earnings visibility. Our top two stock picks are: 1) Public Bank,because earnings remain heavily anchored in its domestic, consumercentric operations. The bank’s strategic focus on domestic retail and SME lending aligns well with expectations of Malaysia-led macro growth in 2025. Asset quality and operational efficiency are key strengths. Currently trading near -1 SD of its 10-year P/B mean, PBB offers a compelling valuation upside, supported by its robust fundamentals and high ROE. 2) Hong Leong Bankas it continues to outperform peers, particularly in consumer and SME loans. Core pre-BOCD profit is supported by healthy contributions from wealth management, global markets, and SME banking. Asset quality remains exceptional, with a low GIL ratio. Strategic partnerships with Lombard Odier and WeBank are expected to further elevate HLBB's wealth and digital banking propositions. Source:TA Research- 10 Sept 2025

Steady Maybank earnings despite softer loan growth Maybank reported a resilient Q2 FY2025, with net profit improving 3.9% YoY to RM2.63b, lifting 1H earnings to RM5.22b, up 4.0% from the prior year. Growth was driven by steady fund-based income (+1.2% YoY) and stronger non-interest income (+7.0% YoY), particularly from trading and wealth management. ROE rose to 11.6% in Q2 (11.5% for 1H), ahead of guidance, though NIM compressed to 2.00% from 2.06% a year ago. Operating costs rose 3.8% YoY as inflationary pressures lifted personnel and marketing spend, pushing the CIR slightly higher to 48.9%. Overall, Maybank’s diversified income and strong CASA ratio of 37.8% provided stability amid softer loan growth of just 1.3% YoY, reflecting client caution under challenging macro conditions. Public Bank delivers resilient topline growth For Public Bank, Q2 net profit came in at RM1.76b, up 0.9% YoY, bringing 1H net profit to RM3.51b (+2.1% YoY). H1 profit before tax rose a stronger 5.3% to RM4.65b, supported by net interest income growth of 4.1% and a notable 17.5% surge in non-interest income, aided by higher investment and FX gains. Operating income advanced 6.8% YoY, but cost pressures lifted opex by 6.7%, nudging the cost-to-income ratio to 35.3% from 34.5% last year. Despite this, PBB remains among the most efficient regional banks. ROE moderated slightly to 12.6% (from 13.2% at end-2024) due to higher capital base, yet still comfortably ahead of Maybank’s. The bank declared a higher first interim dividend of 10.5 sen per share, reflecting a payout ratio of 58.1%, continuing its steady capital return track record. Maybank balance sheet shows resilience but rising risks From a balance sheet perspective, Maybank’s gross loans grew only 1.3% YoY to RM678b, underscoring slower demand across Malaysia and regional markets. Domestic retail loans, led by mortgages (+10.4% YoY), remained the main driver, while Indonesia saw contraction due to corporate deleveraging. Deposits rose 6.1% YoY to RM750b, with CASA improving to 37.8%. Asset quality showed some strain as GIL inched up to 1.30% from 1.23%, mainly from selected corporate and commercial exposures, though loan loss coverage stayed adequate at 117.9%. Credit costs eased to 24bps for 1H, reflecting healthy recoveries. Capital ratios were strong, with CET1 at 14.7% and total capital at 17.9%, supporting a higher interim dividend of 30 sen, equivalent to a payout of 69.5%. Public Bank continues to excel in asset quality Public Bank’s balance sheet dynamics contrasted positively, with loans expanding 5.1% annualised to RM435b and deposits rising 3.5% to RM441b, sustaining its dominant domestic market share. Asset quality remains a hallmark, with GIL steady at 0.54% and credit cost at just 3bps, far superior to peers. Loan loss coverage was robust at 154%, ensuring strong buffers against stress. Sectoral growth was broad-based, led by residential mortgages (+6.0%), commercial properties (+12.0%) and hire purchase (+13.4%). The bank’s capital and liquidity remained solid, with total capital ratio of 16.8% and gross loan-to-fund ratio at 84.1%. Its conservative provisioning and cost discipline underpin resilience, ensuring earnings stability even as fee income from unit trusts softened. This consistent execution strengthens Public Bank’s premium valuation case. Valuation trade-off: Growth volatility vs. structural stability Comparatively, Maybank delivered stronger absolute earnings and higher dividends, reflecting its diversified ASEAN footprint and ability to leverage trading gains. However, its weaker loan momentum, slight asset quality slippage, and margin compression highlight rising headwinds. Public Bank, by contrast, posted steadier albeit slower profit growth, underpinned by impeccable asset quality, industry-leading efficiency, and defensive domestic franchise strength. Investors seeking yield and broader regional exposure may prefer Maybank’s higher payout and scale, while those valuing predictability, pristine credit metrics, and superior cost ratios may lean toward Public Bank. In essence, Maybank offers cyclical upside but with higher risk, whereas Public Bank remains the structural compounder. The relative trade-off between growth volatility and stability will guide positioning into 2H 2025. Asian Stocks Waver Ahead of Key U.S Data Stocks in Asia ended mixed on Tuesday as traders girded for a slate of economic data this week and absorbed the latest developments in trade tensions. Markets widely expect the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates later this month but data this week will help investors gauge whether the central bank could perhaps lean toward a jumbo cut. The focus will be on Friday's U.S. nonfarm payrolls report, which will be preceded by data on job openings and private payrolls, providing investors and the Fed a clearer picture of the labour market that has become the centre of policy debate. Meanwhile, tariff tensions and questions over the Fed’s independence are also compounding the risks in September, historically the weakest month of the year for US markets. Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 slipped 0.30% to 8,900.60, while South Korea’s Kospi rose 0.94% to 3,172.35. Japan’s Nikkei 225 gained 0.29% to 42,310.49 and the Topix added 0.61% to 3,081.88. In the mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index lost 0.45% to 3,858.13, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index slipped 0.47% to 25,496.55. Wall Street Drops as Historically Weak Month Begins Wall Street’s major indexes fell overnight, kicking off what is historically the worst trading month of the year as traders braced for a pivotal jobs report that could recalibrate expectations for upcoming interest-rate cuts. The Dow Jones Industrial Average ended down 0.55% to close at 45,295.81. The S&P 500 dropped 0.69% to settle at 6,415.54, while the Nasdaq Composite slid 0.82% to close at 21,279.63. Traders are focused on Friday’s August jobs report, which could influence expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts. U.S. rate futures widely expect the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates this month, pricing in a 92% chance of a 25-basis-point cut at the end of the two-day policy meeting on September 17, according to CME Group's Fed Watch. Traders are also watching Washington, where a court hearing on whether Trump can fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook ended without a ruling, even as he seeks to reshape the Board of Governors. Besides, in a major blow to Trump’s trade agenda, a federal appeals court ruled most of his global tariffs unconstitutional. Data going back decades shows that, on average, September is the worst month for U.S. stocks, and some investors are bracing for another bumpy ride this year. General dollar strength and uncertainty surrounding Japanese politics likely contributed to the moves. (Bloomberg) Trump Says Not Considering Lowering Tariffs on India Imports President Donald Trump says he’s not looking at lowering tariffs on India, one week after the US doubled levies on the country’s imports to 50% as punishment for its Russian oil imports. Trump responded “no” when asked by a reporter whether he is considering taking away some of the tariffs imposed on India. “We get along with India very well,” Trump added Tuesday at a White House event, but criticized what he said was an imbalanced trade relationship with New Delhi because of high Indian levies. “India has, you have to understand, for many years, it was a one-sided relationship,” he said. “India was charging us tremendous tariffs, about the highest in the world.” The US tariffs on India came despite months of negotiations between New Delhi and Washington and stunned officials in the Asian nation. India’s high tariffs and protectionist policies have exasperated US trade negotiators. Trump set a 25% duty on Indian exports but doubled that level to 50% last week as punishment for purchases of Russian oil. Those levies hit more than 55% of goods shipped to the US, which is India’s biggest market. (Bloomberg) Korea’s Inflation Eases, Giving BOK More Room to Resume Cuts South Korea’s consumer inflation cooled to the slowest pace this year, giving the central bank more room to resume its monetary easing cycle at its next policy meeting in October. Consumer prices rose 1.7% from a year earlier, slowing from July’s 2.1% gain, the statistics office said Tuesday. Economists surveyed by Bloomberg had forecast the pace of price growth would ease to 1.9%. The slowdown was mainly due to a one-time drop in communications costs for some users. A gauge of prices that excludes food and energy rose 1.3% in August, compared with a 2% pace in July, according to Statistics Korea. That result was well below the 1.7% estimate and marked the slowest in four years. Price growth eased as rising food costs were offset by a more than 13% drop in communications costs after SK Telecom Co. slashed in half mobile bills for more than 20 million subscribers affected by a data breach. That temporary impact will fade in September, likely setting the stage for overall price growth to nudge higher again. (Bloomberg) US Manufacturing Mired in Weakness; AI Spending Boom Helps Some Factories US manufacturing contracted for a sixth straight month in August as factories continued to grapple with the impact of import tariffs, but an artificial intelligence spending boom is lending support to some segments of the industry. The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) said on Tuesday its manufacturing PMI edged up to 48.7 last month from 48.0 in July. A PMI reading below 50 indicates contraction in manufacturing, which accounts for 10.2% of the economy. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast the PMI would rise to 49.0. Despite the persistent weakness in the PMI, businesses have been boosting spending on AI products, which is helping to offset some of the drag from import duties. The ISM survey's forwardlooking new orders sub-index increased to 51.4 after contracting for six consecutive months. (Reuters) US Construction Spending Dips in July U.S. construction spending fell in July as high mortgage rates continued to constrain the housing market. The Commerce Department's Census Bureau said on Tuesday that construction spending slipped 0.1% after an unrevised 0.4% drop in June. The decline was in line with economists' expectations. Spending dropped 2.8% on a year-over-year basis in July. Spending on private construction projects fell 0.2%. Investment in residential construction gained 0.1%, with outlays on new single-family housing projects nudging up 0.1%. Residential investment contracted in the second quarter at its fastest pace in 2-1/2 years. It is expected to decrease further in the third quarter, which would mark the third straight quarterly decline. (The Edge, Bernama) Eurozone Inflation Quickens Beyond 2%, Backing ECB Rate Pause Euro-area inflation accelerated beyond the European Central Bank’s target, cementing expectations that officials will keep interest rates steady when they meet next week. Consumer prices rose 2.1% from a year ago in August, edging up from 2% the previous month and meeting the estimates of economists in a Bloomberg poll. A core measure that strips out volatile components like energy and food held at 2.3%. Closely watched gains in services prices eased to 3.1%. The report will confirm the ECB’s view that it can take another break from lowering borrowing costs on Sept 11, comfortable with both the pace of inflation and the economy’s ability to withstand higher US trade levies. (Bloomberg) UK Borrowing Costs Hit Highest Since 1998, Pound Slides on Fiscal Worries Britain's 30-year borrowing costs rose to their highest levels since 1998 and sterling slid over 1.5% on Tuesday, highlighting growing investor anxiety about the UK's ability to keep its finances under control. The sell-off in British bonds, known as gilts, coincided with selling across major bond markets where the focus is firmly on rising debt levels. But weakness in sterling — set for its biggest one-day fall since 2023 — pointed to vulnerability in UK markets at a time of increasing concern about the Labour government's ability to exercise fiscal constraint. Thirty-year gilt yields rose to 5.697%, their highest since May 1998. Sterling was last down 1.1% at US$1.34 and was 0.5% softer at 86.98 pence per euro, by far the weakest performing G10 currency against the dollar. (Bloomberg) Source:TA Research- 3 Sept 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (Aug 26): Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK), Malaysia’s third-largest bank by assets, said on Tuesday its net profit slipped in the second quarter from a year earlier, dragged by lower non-taxable income. Net profit for the three months ended June 30, 2025 (2QFY2025) was RM1.76 billion, a 1% decline when compared to RM1.78 billion recorded for the same period last year, Public Bank said in an exchange filing. Year-on-year, net interest income rose 5.1% while non-interest income climbed 15% during the quarter. The bank declared a first interim dividend of 10.5 sen per share, payable on Sept 24. The entitlement date is Sept 12. “Public Bank continues to ensure that it remains well-capitalised at all times to support its business growth, while optimising returns to its stakeholders,” the bank said, noting that its capital and liquidity position remained healthy while asset quality was resilient with prudent loan loss reserves. The group will continue to proactively grow its business leveraging on its core strength in the retail banking segment, whilst maintaining a strong focus on environmental, social and governance (ESG) initiatives and digital transformation to drive business growth, said its managing director and chief executive officer Tan Sri Dr Tay Ah Lek. For the first half of FY2025, Public Bank’s net profit rose 2.05% to RM3.51 billion from RM3.44 a year earlier as net interest income grew by 4.1% year-on-year while non-interest income grew 17.5%. Non-interest and non-financing income growth was further complemented by the newly acquired general insurance business undertaken by LPI Capital Bhd (KL:LPI). As at end-June, the bank’s asset quality remained stable with the gross impaired loans ratio standing at 0.5% and credit costs at three basis points, well below the banking industry’s average. Loan loss coverage stood at a prudent level of 153.9%, exceeding the 90.5% industry average. Public Bank’s common equity Tier 1 capital — a measure of a bank’s capital strength based on the highest quality of regulatory capital — stood at 14%. Moving forward, the bank said its outlook is weighed by uncertainties which could lead to heightened volatility in global financial markets and commodity prices. Investment momentum is expected to be sustained by the progress of multi-year projects in both the private and public sectors, providing buffers to better absorb external shocks, it added. At Tuesday's noon break, Public Bank's shares were down half a sen or 1.11% at RM4.46, giving the group a market capitalisation of RM86.57 billion. Source: TheEdge - 27 Aug 2025
Source:RHB Research- 27 Aug 2025

Trump’s Trade Offensive and Fed Turmoil Rattle Asian Markets Asian markets fell Tuesday, as traders digested U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest round of tariff threats and his abrupt decision to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook. The U.S. President has threatened China with 200% tariffs if it does not export rare-earth magnets to the U.S, amid an ongoing trade dispute between the two nations. He has also vowed to impose “substantial” new tariffs and curb U.S. chip exports to countries that enforce digital taxes, legislation and regulations that affect American tech companies. Market sentiment faced additional pressure after President Trump dismissed Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook from her post over mortgage fraud allegations. South Korea’s Kospi fell 0.95% to close at 3,179.36, while Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 lost 0.41% to 8,935.60. Japan’s Nikkei 225 dropped 0.97% to 42,394.40 and the Topix sank 1.08% to 3,071.99. In the mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index edged lower by 0.39% to 3,868.38, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index slid 1.18% to end at 25,524.92. Wall Street Edges Higher Ahead of Nvidia Results Wall Street’s major indexes nudged higher in choppy trade overnight, as traders shifted their attention to Nvidia’s upcoming earnings and brushed aside the latest Fed controversy sparked by Trump. The Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 0.30% to finish at 45,418.07. The S&P 500 added 0.41% to 6,465.94, while Nasdaq Composite rose 0.44% to end the day at 21,544.27. The choppy trading seen for most of the day came as traders seemed reluctant to make significant moves ahead of earnings news from Nvidia. The AI darling and market leader, which due to report its second quarter results after the close of trading on Wednesday, ended the day up by 1.1%. Traders are treading carefully after Trump said he is removing Fed Governor Lisa Cook from her position amid accusations she made false statements on one or more mortgage agreements. However, Cook responded with a statement claiming Trump does not have the authority to fire her and said she will not resign. The latest U.S. economic data is also likely to attract attention later this week, as the Commerce Department is due to release a report on Friday that includes the Fed's preferred readings on consumer price inflation. (New Straits Times) Economy Malaysia committed to addressing AI energy demands through clean energy collaboration, says Fadillah Malaysia is committed to working with members of the Clean Energy Ministerial (CEM) to address energy issues involving artificial intelligence (AI), said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof. He told a high-level dialogue titled “Energy and AI” that the rapid growth of data centres and AI-driven applications is creating new, concentrated demands for electricity. "Meeting these demands sustainably requires not only scaling up renewable energy integration and strengthening our grids, but also investing in innovative cooling and storage technologies to ensure reliability and affordability," he said at the 16th CEM and Mission Innovation 10 (MI10) in Busan, South Korea, on Tuesday. Malaysia, as part of the 21-member economies in the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (Apec), has been invited for an intervention during the CEM16/MI-10 forum, held alongside the Apec Energy Ministerial Meeting and the World Climate Industry Expo 2025. (The Edge, Bernama) US Yet to Offer Further Clarity on Transshipment Rules — Miti Transshipment activities have not yet been fully clarified in terms of enforcement implementation as the United States (US) has not outlined in detail the categories of such activities, said the Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry (Miti). Miti, in a written reply published on the Parliament website on Tuesday, said that on July 31, 2025, US President Donald Trump announced a new executive order on reciprocal tariff rates, with Malaysia being imposed a 19% rate effective Aug 7, 2025. “The latest US executive order stipulates that goods identified by the United States Customs and Border Protection (CBP) can be subject to tariffs of up to 40% instead of the original reciprocal rate,” it said. The ministry noted that transshipment activities do not conflict with the laws of any country as they are part of a global business strategy. “However, transshipment involving the abuse of Certificate of Origin (COO) to circumvent or avoid import duties is an act that violates regulations,” it said, replying to a question from Senator Rita Sarimah Patrick Insol regarding the impact of US tariffs on the volume of transshipment cargo handled at Malaysia’s main ports. (The Edge) Plantation Ministry submitting SST exemption request for crude palm kernel oil, palm kernel olein to MOF, Parliament hears The Ministry of Plantation and Commodities is submitting a request to the Ministry of Finance (MOF) for crude palm kernel oil (CPKO) and palm kernel olein to be exempted from the sales and service tax (SST), both of which are currently taxed at a rate of 5%. Deputy Plantation and Commodities Minister Datuk Chan Foong Hin said the ministry had reviewed feedback from its engagement sessions with industry players on the impact of SST implementation. “The exemption is reasonable as both are raw materials in the production of oleochemical products and not finished goods, which are subject to government tax. Proposals related to SST exemptions or relief for certain critical services essential to industry operations have also been considered to ease cost pressures,” he said during a question-and-answer session in the Dewan Rakyat. (The Edge, Bernama) Malaysia to Become Aged Nation by 2048, Says Amir Hamzah Malaysia is projected to become an ‘aged nation’ by 2048, when the proportion of citizens aged 65 and above reaches 14% of the total population, Finance Minister II Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan said. Amir Hamzah, carrying out the duties and functions of the economy minister, said this projection is based on the latest statistics from the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) as of July this year. He said Malaysia officially became an ageing nation in 2021, when the proportion of those aged 65 and above reached 7% of the total population. “Increased life expectancy and declining fertility rates mean that the number of younger people aged between zero and 14 will continue to decrease, while the elderly population above 65 will keep rising,” he told the Dewan Rakyat during question time on Tuesday. (The Edge, Bernama) MOF Projects Impact of SST Implementation on Households to be Low, Manageable The overall impact of sales and service tax (SST) implementation on households is estimated to be low and manageable, based on early Ministry of Finance (MOF) projections, said Deputy Finance Minister Lim Hui Ying. Lim elaborated that this is due to various relief measures and exemptions introduced as mitigation steps to ensure the tax burden is fairly distributed and borne by those with the ability. “It is still too early to assess the full impact of SST implementation on the public, given that the expanded scope of the SST only began on July 1. However, based on the ministry’s initial projections, the impact on households is estimated to be low and manageable,” she told the Dewan Negara during a question-and-answer session on Tuesday. Lim was responding to Senator Datuk Ros Suryati Alang, who asked about the effects of SST implementation from July 1 on the public, and whether the SST strategy had helped the government cover its operational and development expenses without increasing loans or debt. (The Edge, Bernama) Finance Ministry Says No Plans to Reintroduce GST for Now There are no plans to reintroduce the Goods and Services Tax (GST) anytime soon in order to protect the interests of the people and businesses, says the Finance Ministry. The ministry said this is because the current general income level is too low, leaving people financially vulnerable if subjected to a broad-based tax like GST. It added that continuing with the current Sales and Service Tax (SST) system provides a quicker fiscal impact for the government compared to bringing back GST. “Bringing back GST would require a much longer preparation period—up to two years—to allow companies to update their systems and prepare for GST compliance. SST is also more progressive compared to reinstating GST, as the expanded SST structure is targeted, and commonly consumed goods and services are generally not taxed,” the ministry said. The ministry added that the current expansion of service tax covers 70% of services listed under the Malaysia Standard Industrial Classification (MSIC) code, compared to 76% under GST. (The Star) No Property Oversupply in KL, No Residential Project Moratorium, Says Zaliha There is no oversupply of property in Kuala Lumpur and there will be no moratorium on residential projects in the city, says Datuk Seri Dr Zaliha Mustafa. She said in reply to a question from Young Syefura Othman (PH–Bentong) that the notion of an oversupply of property in Kuala Lumpur is not entirely accurate, as there was a 10.3% decrease in unsold properties compared to previous years. The Minister in the Prime Minister’s Department (Federal Territories) imposing a moratorium on housing projects would have several negative implications, including a decline in Kuala Lumpur’s Gross Domestic Product (GDP), which relies heavily on the construction sector and property-related services. Young Syefura had asked if the government plans to impose a moratorium on residential projects in Kuala Lumpur given Department of Statistics projections that the city will reach its peak population in 2031 This is 28 years earlier than the national projection. (The Star) China to Expand Carbon Market With Major Industries by 2027 China is accelerating the expansion of its carbon market with a plan to incorporate major industrial sectors by 2027. The national carbon emissions trading market will be largely established by 2030, according to guidelines issued by the Communist Party Central Committee and the State Council. The move will help deliver President Xi Jinping’s pledge to peak China’s emissions by the end of the decade. China’s carbon market was launched four years ago, covering only power generators, the country’s main source of emissions. Three other heavily polluting industries — steel, aluminium and cement — will be added this year, although they’ll be subject to less stringent rules in their first two years of inclusion. (Bloomberg) Japan Ministry to Request Record Sum for Debt-servicing Costs, Draft Shows Japan's finance ministry is planning to request a record US$220bn for debt-servicing costs in next financial year's budget as the world's number four economy faces rising interest rates, according to a draft document seen by Reuters. The ballooning debt costs highlight Japan's struggle to rein in spending, when it can no longer rely on ultra-low borrowing costs and on the Bank of Japan to effectively bankroll debt as the central bank shifts away from its decadelong stimulus programme. The debt-servicing costs for interest payments and debt redemption for the year starting in April would total JPY32.4tn, exceeding JPY28.2tn allocated for the current financial year, the draft showed. The assumed interest rate would increase to 2.6% for the next financial year from the current year's final budget assumed rate of 2.0%, hitting the highest level in 17 years, according to sources familiar with the matter. (Reuters) Korea’s Consumer Sentiment Rises to Seven-year High Ahead of BOK Rate Decision South Korea’s consumer confidence rose to the highest in seven years as surging stock prices and resilient exports offset uncertainties surrounding US tariffs, giving the central bank added incentive to hold policy steady this week. The composite consumer sentiment index climbed 0.6 point to 111.4 in August, the highest reading since January 2018, according to a Bank of Korea report released Tuesday. The survey-based index remains well above the neutral threshold of 100, signalling continued optimism. Confidence has now increased for a fifth consecutive month as the nation continues to recover from political turmoil at the start of the year. (Bloomberg) Singapore Industrial Output Growth Steady at 7.1% Singapore's industrial production expanded at a stable pace in July, figures from the Economic Development Board showed Tuesday. Industrial output grew 7.1% year-on-year in July, the same as in June. Production has been rising since August 2024. Excluding biomedical manufacturing, production advanced 9.4%. Among clusters, transport engineering output surged 15.8%, and electronics production climbed 13.1%. Precision engineering expanded 9.6%, and chemicals output rose 4.2%. On the other hand, general manufacturing output fell notably by 9.7% from last year. On a monthly basis, manufacturing output rebounded sharply by 8.2% versus a 0.8% decrease in June. (RTT) US Home Prices Increase at Slowest Rate Since 2023 Home-price growth in the US slowed for a fifth straight month in June. A national gauge of prices rose 1.9% from a year earlier, according to data from S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller. That was the smallest gain since the summer of 2023 and followed a 2.3% increase in May. The US is coming off its weakest spring selling season in 13 years after high prices and mortgage rates sidelined many would-be buyers. With few eager shoppers in the hunt, sellers are warming up to discounts and other concessions in parts of the country where listings have piled up. While that has contributed to a nationwide easing of price growth, some areas — such as New York — are still fiercely competitive. Among 20 major cities, New York still “stands as a stark outlier,” Godec said, leading the index with a 7% annual gain in prices. Following were Chicago, with a 6.1% increase, and Cleveland, at 4.5%. (Bloomberg) US Core Capital Goods Orders Rise, Exceeding All Forecasts US orders for business equipment increased in July by more than projected, suggesting companies are moving forward on investment plans as uncertainty around trade and tax policy gradually diminishes. The value of core capital goods orders, a proxy for investment in equipment that excludes aircraft and military hardware, increased 1.1% last month after a revised 0.6% decrease in June, Commerce Department figures showed Tuesday. The gain exceeded all forecasts in a Bloomberg survey of economists. Bookings for all durable goods — items meant to last at least three years and including orders for commercial aircraft and military equipment — fell 2.8%. Earlier this month, Boeing Co reported fewer orders in July than in June. Despite the gain, economists expect business investment to be soft for the remainder of the year before picking up in 2026 as companies take advantage of tax provisions after President Donald Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill. (Bloomberg) US Consumer Confidence Falls Slightly on Job, Income Concerns US consumer confidence fell slightly in August as Americans worried more about their prospects of finding a job. The Conference Board’s gauge of sentiment decreased 1.3 points to 97.4 after an upward revision to the prior month, data out Tuesday showed. The median estimate in a Bloomberg survey of economists called for a reading of 96.5. A measure of expectations for the next six months declined in August, while present conditions decreased to the lowest since April. Consumer confidence continues to hover well below levels seen prior to the pandemic. The recent labour-market slowdown has added to economic concerns stemming from President Donald Trump’s tariffs. Job growth and wage gains have significantly slowed, and it’s become increasingly difficult for unemployed Americans to find a new a role. (Bloomberg) Tariff Uncertainty Leaves European Companies With Tough Decisions When the Washington Commanders’ quarterback Marcus Mariota landed a last-second touchdown pass to clinch victory against the Dallas Cowboys in January, it’s unlikely devastated Cowboys fans were thinking about turf underlay, or the small Dutch town where it’s made. Privately-owned TenCate Grass, based in Nijverdal, about two hours drive east of Amsterdam, engineers artificial grass for gardens, schools and sports grounds, from municipal facilities to the Cowboys’ AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas. The firm manufactures in 11 countries and distributes turf to customers in more than 60, but around 75% of its sales come from the US. That means it’s exposed to American trade policy, which, since US President Donald Trump took office in January, has been erratic. On so-called “Liberation Day”, April 2, Trump announced a blanket 20% tariff on EU goods coming into the US. That was dropped to 10% while the parties negotiated — during which time, Trump threatened higher rates of 30% and 50%. (Bloomberg) Source:TA Research- 27 Aug 2025

Asian Markets Close Higher on U.S. Data Cues Asian markets recovered from early declines to finish in positive territory Wednesday, as traders weighed softer-than-expected economic data for insights into the future path of interest rates. A weaker-than-expected report on activity for U.S. businesses in services industries like transportation and retail added to worries that Trump’s tariffs may be hurting the economy. Nevertheless, conversely such indicators raise hopes the Federal Reserve may cut interest rates. Expectations have built sharply for a rate cut in September meeting since a report on the U.S. job market on Friday came in much weaker than economists expected. Lower interest rates would make stocks look less expensive, while also giving the overall economy a boost. The potential downside is that they could push inflation higher. On economic news, Japan's real wages fell in June for the sixth consecutive month as inflation continued to outpace pay growth, government data showed on Wednesday, raising concerns about consumption-led recovery in the world's fourth-largest economy. Japan’s Nikkei 225 gained 0.60% to finish at 40,794.86, while the Topix climbed 1.02% to 2,966.57. Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 rose 0.84% to 8,843.70 and South Korea’s Kospi ended nearly unchanged at 3,198.14. In the mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index also gained 0.45% to 3,633.99, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index inched higher by 0.03% to 24,910.63. Wall Street Gains on Apple Optimism Wall Street's major indexes climbed overnight, led by a more than 1% gain in the Nasdaq after Apple unveiled a bold domestic manufacturing pledge, fueling optimism across tech and broader markets. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.18% to 44,193.12. The S&P 500 gained 0.73% to close at 6,345.06, while the Nasdaq Composite advanced 1.21% and settled at 21,169.42. Shares of Apple jumped and provided the biggest boost to all three of the major indexes after a White House official said the company would announce a USD100 billion domestic manufacturing pledge. Other large cap tech names such as Amazon, Tesla and Broadcom were all more than 3% higher. On earnings front, McDonald's stock rose after the company reversed its US sales slump. And Uber announced a revenue beat along with a USD20 billion stock buyback. Earnings season continues with companies reporting strong results, with about 81% of S&P 500 companies that have reported to date trouncing expectations, according to FactSet. (The Edge, Bernama) Chicken Prices Still Stable After Removal of Subsidies, Dewan Rakyat Told Chicken prices have remained stable since the removal of subsidies on Nov 1, 2023, the Dewan Rakyat was told on Wednesday. Deputy Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh said that prior to the subsidy removal, the market faced issues with price control despite the government having set a subsidised price cap. "One of the strategies we implemented was to clamp down on chicken feed cartels, as chicken feed accounts for 70% of production costs. So, we focused on controlling production costs. Since Nov 1, 2023, the ministry has been collecting daily chicken price data to ensure the approach is truly effective. Although there are no longer any subsidies, we regulate the supply chain and monitor chicken prices and to date, prices have stabilised and in some cases are even lower,” she said. Commenting further, Fuziah said a similar situation was also recorded for the egg market following the termination of egg subsidies effective Aug 1. (The Edge, Bernama) Skills-related Underemployment Drops to 35.7% in 1Q2025 — Deputy HR Minister Malaysia’s rate of skills-related underemployment declined to 35.7% in the first quarter of 2025, down from 37.3% in 2022, the Dewan Rakyat was told on Wednesday. Deputy Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri Abdul Rahman Mohamad said the decrease was the result of coordinated efforts across ministries to address skills mismatches in the labour market. “The Ministry of Human Resources (Kesuma), through agencies such as TalentCorp, the Manpower Department (JTM) and the Human Resource Development Corporation (HRD Corp), plays a key role in developing a skilled workforce that meets current industry demands,” he said during the question-and-answer session. He was responding to Mohd Misbahul Munir Masduki (PN– Parit Buntar), who asked about steps taken to address labour market imbalances, particularly with 35.7% of current unemployment linked to skills mismatch. Abdul Rahman said 84,231 individuals underwent training under JTM programmes in 2024, with 48,833 trained as of June 30. Global trade challenges continue to linger but prospects for the Indian economy remain "bright", Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Governor Sanjay Malhotra said in a statement. The six-member rate-setting panel voted unanimously to hold the key repo rate at 5.50% and decided to continue with a “neutral” policy stance. While headline inflation is much lower than expected, it is largely due to volatile food prices and is set to rise towards the end of the year, Malhotra said. A large majority of economists, 44 of 57, had forecast a pause in a July 18-24 Reuters poll, following a surprise 50-basis-point cut in June. The central bank has cut the policy repo rate by 100 basis points (bps) so far in 2025 as price pressures eased. cuts. (Reuters) Vietnam Trade Beats Estimates as Buyers Race Trump Tariffs Vietnam’s exports jumped more than expected in July, with buyers racing to avoid a 20% tariff on the country’s exports to the US, set to take effect on Aug 7. Exports rose 16% in July from a year earlier to US$42.3bn (RM178.81bn), the statistics office said in a statement, beating expectations of 14% growth. Imports rose 17.8% in the period to US$40bn, more than the 15.2% forecast. The trade surplus was US$2.27bn, versus US$2.83bn released in June. The Southeast Asian nation, an export powerhouse that sells everything from coffee and clothing to engine parts, has been shipping more goods this year to buyers aiming to avoid US President Donald Trump’s tariffs. The country was initially threatened with a 46% import levy, though that has been lowered to 20%, just one percentage point more than for neighbours Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand. (Bloomberg) Trump Says ‘getting Very Close’ on Extending China Trade Truce US President Donald Trump said he is “getting very close to a deal” with China to extend the trade truce that saw the two countries agree to reduce tit-for-tat tariff hikes and ease export restrictions on rare earth magnets and certain technologies. “It’s not imperative, but I think we are going to make a good deal,” Trump said in an interview with CNBC, adding that the US is “getting along with China very well”. Still, Trump downplayed the notion that he is eager for a meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping, saying he would only want to see his Chinese counterpart as part of an effort to conclude trade negotiations. “I will end up having a meeting before the end of the year, most likely, if we make a deal,” Trump said. The preliminary deal between the US and China is set to expire on Aug 12. That initial truce eased worries of a tariff war that threatened to choke off bilateral trade between the world’s two largest economies and also gave the countries more time to discuss other unresolved issues such as duties tied to fentanyl trafficking. Last week, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng met in Stockholm — the third round of trade talks between the US and Beijing in less than three months. (Reuters) Trump Says US Will Charge Tariff of About 100% on Semiconductor Imports The United States will impose a tariff of about 100% on semiconductor chips imported into the country, President Donald Trump said on Wednesday. Trump told reporters in the Oval Office that the new tariff rate would apply to "all chips and semiconductors coming into the United States," but would not apply to companies that had made a commitment to manufacture in the United States. "So, 100% tariff on all chips and semiconductors coming into the United States. But if you've made a commitment to build (in the U.S.), or if you're in the process of building (in the U.S.), as many are, there is no tariff," Trump said. (Reuters) Canada's Carney Says He Will Look at Opportunities to Remove Tariffs on US Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada will look at whether to remove some countertariffs against the US, despite winning an election in which he pledged to fight back against US President Donald Trump’s trade war. Carney said retaliatory tariffs should be designed to maximise the impact on the US but minimise the pain for Canadian businesses. When the US changes its tariff rate, “we don’t automatically adjust. We look at what we can do for our industry that’s most effective”, he said in West Kelowna, British Columbia. “In some cases, that will be to remove tariffs. We have removed some tariffs over time so that, for example, the auto industry can function more effectively. And we will look at opportunities to do so.” The comments underscore a politically awkward position for Carney as he seeks to minimise economic damage while also standing firm against Trump. The US president has imposed a broadside of tariffs and infuriated many Canadians by saying their country should be a 51st US state. Polls show there’s strong appetite among the Canadian public for a hardball approach to the trade talks. (Bloomberg) Eurozone Retail Sales Recover in June Euro area retail sales rebounded in June suggesting that household spending likely supported economic growth in the second quarter, official data revealed on Wednesday. Retail sales grew 0.3% on a monthly basis in June, offsetting May's 0.3% decline, Eurostat said. However, this was marginally weaker than economists' forecast for a 0.4% increase. Food, drink and tobacco sales rose 0.2% and non-food product sales gained 0.6%. Sales of automotive fuel in specialized stores grew 0.4% from a month ago. On a yearly basis, Eurozone retail sales growth accelerated to 3.1% from 1.9% in May. Economists were expecting an annual growth of 2.6%. Retail sales in the EU27 also grew 0.3% on a monthly basis in June and 3.1% from the previous year. (RTT) G-20 Needs to Fix Debt Revamp Framework — Top African Banker The Group of 20 should consider improving its debt restructuring framework for poor nations, the former vice president of the African Development Bank said. The G-20 needs to “think about debt restructuring frameworks and how to make them work,” Swazi Tshabalala told a conference in Johannesburg on Wednesday discussing the cost of capital, adding that the bloc needs to commit to firm timelines for implementation. The G-20’s Common Framework, established in 2020 to provide a coordinated approach to restructuring unsustainable debt for low-income countries, has been criticised for being too slow and politically fraught. Some debt reworks, such as those in Zambia and Ghana, have dragged on for years. It also doesn’t address nations saddled by high debt-service costs. Her comments were made as South Africa is set to host leaders from the G-20 at a summit in November as part of its presidency of the bloc. Africa’s largest economy has made easing the heavy debt burden of low-income nations a priority. Tshabalala also urged multilateral institutions to collaborate to fast-track development on the continent, which needs US$1.3tn annually by the end of 2030 to meet its sustainable development needs, at a time when the US and European nations are cutting aid. (Bloomberg) Source:TA Research- 7 Aug 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (July 17): Maybank Investment Bank (Maybank IB) has slashed its FBM KLCI earnings growth forecast to 2.5% for 2025 from 6.1% previously, owing to a weak banking outlook. Banking sector earnings, which account for about half of the FBM KLCI’s earnings base, are now projected to grow by a mere 1% this year against the previous estimate of 5.5%, according to Maybank IB head of equity research Lim Sue Lin. “Following the 25-basis-point cut in the overnight policy rate, plus the fact of a softer macro environment, we lowered the loan growth [target] and raised credit costs by about 20% across all banks under our coverage. This resulted in the earnings growth [forecast] for the banking sector [dropping] to 1% from about 5.5%. “Our overall earnings growth [projection] for the FBM KLCI would be now at 2.5% from 6.1% previously,” Lim said at the firm’s second half of 2025 (2H2025) market outlook briefing here on Thursday. Nonetheless, she expects a better earnings growth of 7.7% in 2026 after an expected lower earnings base in 2025. As such, the FBM KLCI’s 2025 year-end target is kept at 1,660 points, assuming easing trade tensions and a favourable tariff negotiation outcome, she added. Sector-wise, Maybank IB remains positive on domestic-centric consumer plays, as it expects domestic policy tailwinds to keep the Malaysian economy on a growth path. Besides the consumer segment, the bank remains positive on sectors like healthcare, real estate investment trust (REIT) and renewable energy. Healthcare, particularly hospital operators, is favoured for the expected ramp-up of both organic and inorganic expansion plans as well as the delay in the rollout of the diagnostic-related group payment system. However, it cautioned that a potential pushback from insurance and takaful operators — on the back of pricing negotiations or revoking cashless payment for policyholders — may weigh on patient volumes. Meanwhile, Maybank IB said the REIT sector is supported by positive rental reversions, improved occupancy rates and contributions from new asset acquisitions. As for renewable energy, it sees a few key supporting factors, such as strong execution of the corporate green power programme, stabilising solar panel prices and the upcoming rollout of the large-scale solar (LSS5+ and LSS6) initiatives. Lim said the bank has raised its conviction on the construction sector for 2H2025, driven by build-up activities in the data centre space and a robust infrastructure pipeline. While maintaining a neutral stance on plantation, Maybank IB sees selective opportunities in the sector, noting that selected planters with suitable and well-located land near the national grid and interconnection points could have the opportunity to participate in the LSS projects. Also, the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone may augur well for plantation players who have land there. Westport Holdings Bhd’s (KL:WPRTS) earnings visibility is also expected to be lifted by tariff hikes and higher storage fees. Maybank IB’s top picks are Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK), AMMB Holdings Bhd (KL:AMBANK), YTL Power International Bhd (KL:YTLPOWR), Tenaga Nasional Bhd (KL:TENAGA), Gamuda Bhd (KL:GAMUDA), Frontken Corp Bhd (KL:FRONTKN), ITMAX System Bhd (KL:ITMAX), Aurelius Technologies Bhd (KL:ATECH), Farm Fresh Bhd (KL:FFB), Solarvest Holdings Bhd (KL:SLVEST) and Pavilion REIT (KL:PAVREIT). Ringgit to end 2025 at 4.10 against US dollar On the currency front, Maybank head of FX Research Saktiandi Supaat is mildly bullish on the ringgit outlook, driven by the continued softening of the US dollar and portfolio inflows into Malaysian bonds. The local currency is forecast to end the year at RM4.10 against the greenback, before strengthening further to RM4 by end-2026. Having gained more than 5% against the US dollar since the beginning of the year, the ringgit was trading at RM4.2490 at the time of writing on Thursday. Source: TheEdge - 18 Jul 2025

BNM Cut OPR to 2.75% On Wednesday, Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) lowered the Overnight Policy Rate (OPR) by 25 basis points to 2.75%, from 3.00%—its first rate cut in nearly two years. The move, which was largely anticipated, comes after the policy rate had remained unchanged since May 2023. According to BNM, the decision is a pre-emptive step to support growth amid rising downside risks to the global outlook, despite the domestic economy remaining relatively resilient. With inflation expected to stay moderate, the cut is aimed at maintaining a stable growth trajectory. Will Lower Rates Translate Into Higher Loan Growth? Nevertheless, the lower rate environment could provide some offsetting benefits through improved sentiment and potentially stronger credit demand. In this thematic report, we assess the potential impact of loan growth by revisiting historical periods of OPR cuts to examine trends in residential mortgage and hire purchase (HP) loan growth. Together, these two segments account for roughly 82% of total consumer loans, and as such, they could serve as a valuable barometer for rate sensitivity. Automotive Sector (by Angeline Chin) OPR Cuts Limited Impact on Automotive Sales The recent 25bps cut in the OPR is generally aimed at encouraging borrowing by lowering loan costs, which in turn support consumer spending. However, our analysis indicates that the impact on the automotive sector will be limited. Based on car loan amounts ranging from RM34,580 to RM355,888 and repayment periods of 5 to 9 years, the monthly repayment savings would only range between RM6 and RM67. This amount is unlikely to influence consumer purchasing decisions for vehicles significantly. Assessment of OPR Reduction's Impact Historically, reductions in the OPR have shown a limited direct impact on boosting automotive sales in Malaysia. Broader economic conditions, consumer sentiment, and specific government incentives have consistently played a more significant role in influencing Total Industry Volume (TIV). Global Financial Crisis (2008-2009):Despite a substantial OPR cut from 3.5% to 2.0% between November 2008 and February 2009, TIV still saw a 2% decline in 2009. This was primarily due to the severe impact of the global financial crisis on the Malaysian economy, resulting in a significant dent in consumer confidence. Economic Slowdown (2016):An OPR reduction from 3.25% to 3.00% in 2016 also failed to bolster auto sales, with TIV falling 13% YoY. This period was characterised by a broader economic slowdown, a weaker Ringgit, tighter lending conditions, and subdued consumer sentiment, all of which overshadowed any potential benefits from lower interest rates. COVID-19 Pandemic (2020):During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, a sharp OPR cut from 3% to 1.75% coincided with a 12.4% drop in TIV. The primary factor for this decline was the implementation of the Movement Control Order (MCO), which severely restricted economic activity. While monthly TIV did surge later, this was predominantly driven by the government's Sales Tax Exemption (SST exemption) incentive, rather than the OPR cuts. Recommendation Accordingly, we do not anticipate that this OPR cut will substantially drive an increase in car sales. While it represents a positive gesture, the reduced borrowing costs lack the necessary magnitude to stimulate demand within the automotive sector significantly. As such, we maintain aNEUTRALstance on the sector's outlook. We maintain aSELLrating onMBMR(Target Price: RM4.31, ★★★) andBAUTO(Target Price: RM0.75, ★★★★), whileSIME(Target Price: RM1.66, ★★★★) remains aHOLD. Property Sector (by Thiam Chiann Wen) Why This Matters for Property Developers? The lower OPR will translate into reduced borrowing costs for homebuyers and developers. Assuming banks fully pass on the rate cut to borrowers by lowering their base rates (BR), our analysis shows that a 25bps cut could decrease monthly instalments for a 35-year mortgage by 3.2%. While this reduction alone may not prompt immediate large-scale home purchases, it enhances affordability and could nudge undecided buyers off the sidelines, particularly in the mid-market and mass segments. Should a second rate cut materialise later this year, the cumulative savings on monthly instalments could reach 6.4–6.5%, offering a more meaningful boost to sentiment and affordability. This would strengthen the sector’s cyclical recovery in 2H25 and into 2026. For developers, a lower interest rate environment also improves project viability by easing financing costs, enhancing cash flows, and lifting internal rates of return (IRR). This is especially timely as developers accelerate launches to meet their FY25 sales targets. What History Tells Us: OPR Cuts and Housing Market Momentum Global Financial Crisis (2008-2009):Malaysia’s property market has historically responded well to aggressive rate cuts. When BNMslashed the OPR from 3.50% in late 2008 to 2.00% by early 2009in response to the global financial crisis, residential mortgage applications jumped 33% YoY in 2009 to RM130bn, despite a weak economic backdrop. This marked the beginning of a multi-year recovery, particularly in the affordable and upgrader segments. The momentum continued into the early 2010s, supported not only by accommodative monetary policy but also by strong structural and policy drivers. These included the Economic Transformation Programme, major transport infrastructure such as KVMRT, LRT3 and BRT, and landmark projects like Tun Razak Exchange and Kwasa Damansara. At the same time, investment flows into regional economic corridors, especially Iskandar Malaysia, spurred job creation and migration into emerging townships. The introduction of the Developer Interest Bearing Scheme (DIBS) in 2009 further encouraged demand by lowering upfront payment burdens for buyers. During this period of broad-based policy support and still accommodative interest rates, annual mortgage applications reached RM246bn in 2013, representing a 27% YoY increase. This marked a major peak in the post-global financial crisis cycle, although it was later surpassed during the postCOVID rebound, when applications surged to a record RM390bn in 2021, up 46% from the previous year. Economic Slowdown (2016): However, the upcycle eventually lost momentum following a series of cooling measures. The impact began to show after the announcement of Budget 2014 in October 2013, which introduced stricter regulations for the property market. These included doubling the minimum purchase price for foreigners from RM500,000 to RM1.0mn, banning the DIBS, and raising Real Property Gains Tax rates. At the same time, the OPR was gradually normalised to 3.25% by July 2014. As a result, mortgage applications declined by 9% YoY to RM224bn in 2014. Therefore,the smaller 25bps rate cut in 2016 had limited effect, as affordability pressures, a weaker Ringgit, and tighter lending conditions continued to dampen buyer sentiment. By that point, applications remained 16% below the 2013 peak. COVID-19 Pandemic (2020):During theCOVID period, despite the OPR being lowered to a record low of 1.75%, the property market’s recovery was initially muted due to movement restrictions. Nevertheless, applications rebounded strongly in the second half of 2020 as restrictions eased and buyers took advantage of lower borrowing costs and government incentives. A key catalyst was the PENJANA stimulus package, introduced in June 2020 to support economic recovery. Under the housing segment of PENJANA, the government reintroduced the Home Ownership Campaign (HOC) with full stamp duty exemptions, developer discounts, RPGT waivers, and the removal of the loan-to-value cap for third home purchases. Notably, the average monthly residential loan application during the HOC in 2020 - 2021 was 42% higher than the 2019 campaign. We believe this reflects the combined impact of supportive housing policies and a much lower interest rate environment, with the OPR at 1.75% compared to 3% in 2019. Residential loan applications serve as a useful leading indicator of housing demand, with transaction values typically peaking one to two quarters later. This trend was evident in both past cycles — transactions peaked in 2014 following strong loan activity in 2013, and again in 2022 after mortgage applications surged in 2021. The lag reflects the natural timeline from loan approval to property completion, reinforcing our view that the recent pickup in loan applications should support transaction growth in the coming quarters. Recommendation We maintain ourOverweightcall on the Malaysian property sector heading into 2H25, as share prices remain below their 2024 peaks, presenting investors with a renewed opportunity to accumulate fundamentally strong names at more compelling valuations. The sector is wellpositioned to benefit from a confluence of structural and policy-driven tailwinds, with the recent 25bps OPR cut to 2.75% reinforcing a more supportive financing environment for both homebuyers and developers. Top Buy Picks for the sector areSIMEPROP(Target Price: RM2.05, ★★★★★) andIOIPG(Target Price: RM2.78, ★★★★). Banking Sector (by Li Hsia Wong) Global Financial Crisis (2008-2009):During the GFC, BNM cut the OPR aggressively—from 3.5% in November 2008 to 2.0% by February 2009. Mortgage loan growth held up well, rising from 9.7% YoY to 10.3%, as buyers took advantage of cheaper borrowing costs. Momentum in housing loans remained strong, peaking in 2014 before tapering in 2015 as OPR normalised to 3.25%. Beyond interest rates, we believe other key drivers included the rise of major transport infrastructure, new township developments, and the Developer Interest-Bearing Scheme (DIBS), which significantly boosted buyer affordability and confidence. In contrast, HP loans slowed during the same period, falling from 7.5% YoY in Oct 2008 to 4.8% by end-2009—likely reflecting greater economic uncertainty and cautious discretionary spending. Economic Slowdown (2016):The smaller 25 bps cut in 2016 had a more muted effect. Mortgage growth slowed to 8–9%, weighed down by a softer economy, weak Ringgit, and cautious consumer sentiment. Total Industry Volume (TIV) fell 13% YoY, and auto loans contracted throughout 2016– 2017. This period also marked the beginning of tighter credit conditions, with macroprudential measures introduced to curb rising household debt, including caps on loan tenures and stricter debt service ratio (DSR) requirements. Banks also turned more selective amid concerns over the household debt-to-GDP ratio, which had climbed to 88.4% by 2015. COVID-19 Pandemic (2020):BNM slashed the OPR from 3.00% to a historic low of 1.75% during the pandemic. Despite this, mortgage loan growth remained sluggish at 6–8%, largely constrained by the Movement Control Orders (MCOs), which stalled property transactions. The reintroduction of the Home Ownership Campaign (HOC), however, helped sustain property demand, although it kept mortgage growth steady at around 6–7%. Similarly, HP loans were in contraction mode through mid-2020 amid weak TIV. However, demand picked up after June 2020, when the government introduced the Sales Tax (SST) Exemption from June 2020 to 2022. As a result, HP loan growth rebounded, peaking at 7.7% YoY by May 2021. A Silver Lining for Repayment Trends? One potential silver lining from lower interest rates is improved household cash flow. Based on our estimates in Figures 1 and 5, typical mortgage and HP loan borrowers could see modest savings, which could enhance debt-servicing ability and potentially reduce asset quality risks. To recap, in 1Q CY25, several banks flagged rising mortgage impairments and early signs of stress in unsecured loans, likely reflecting the ongoing impact of elevated living costs. Examining repayment data from August 2004 to May 2025, we observe a negative correlation between OPR and repayment trends, suggesting that as interest rates fall, repayment performance tends to improve. For instance, following the cut from 3.00% in Dec 2019 to 1.75% in Jul 2020, repayments rebounded strongly, rising from YoY contractions in mid-2020 to a peak of 58.8% YoY in Apr 2021. Mildly Negative to Bank Earnings Most banks have announced a 25 bps reduction in their base rate/base lending rate/financing reference rate, in line with the OPR cut. While we reiterate that the recent OPR cut may exert mild pressure on bank earnings due to softer net interest margins, we see the impact as manageable, especially with support from the SRR cut. We also foresee lower borrowing costs to help lift sentiment and encourage lending, although overall loan growth is expected to moderate slightly due to ongoing cost-of-living pressures and weaker external sentiment. Importantly, lower rates may also ease asset quality risks by improving borrowers' cash flow and repayment capacity. With these offsets in place, we maintain ourOVERWEIGHTcall on the banking sector and keep our 2025 loan growth forecast at 5.7% for now. Our top picks for the banking sector includeCIMB(Target Price: RM8.86, ★★★★),PBBANK(Target Price: RM5.15, ★★★★) andHLBANK(Target Price: RM23.59, ★★★★). Final Thoughts: A “Gentle Honk,” Not a “Full-Throttle Rev” While the latest 25 bps cut may offer a short-term lift in sentiment, history suggests it is unlikely to move the needle meaningfully on its own. As our auto analyst puts it, “this feels more like a gentle honk than a full-throttle rev.” The same can be said for housing demand. For any meaningful pickup in mortgage or HP loan growth, we believe that more substantial measures may be required, such as through larger rate cuts, targeted policy incentives (e.g., DIBS, SST exemption) along with broader improvements in consumer confidence, affordability, and credit accessibility. Source:TA Research- 11 Jul 2025

The FBM KLCI shed 7.40 points to finish at 1,530.14, off an early low of 1,526.27 and high of 1,531.14, as losers led gainers 550 to 399 on total turnover of 3.06bn shares worth RM2.2bn. Immediate index support stays at 1,490, with stronger supports found at 1,465 followed by 1,444. Immediate resistance is kept at 1,564 with next upside hurdles seen at the recent high of 1,586, followed by 1,610 ahead. RHB Bank looks attractive to bargain on dips for recovery to RM6.69, with a confirmed breakout to target the 28/03/25 high (RM6.93) ahead, while key supports are at the 61.8%FR (RM6.12) and the 50%FR (RM5.87). Asian Markets Rise on Hopes for Continued Trade Negotiations Asian markets mostly rose on Tuesday, as investors welcomed the delay to the Trump administration’s tariff implementation deadline to August 1, which has left the door open for further trade negotiations. Markets appeared to shrug off the letters shared by President Donald Trump earlier, announcing that several countries in the region are set to face steep tariff rates after the deadline passes. Furthermore, President Trump’s comments that the August 1 deadline was “not 100% firm” also added to optimism for potential trade deals with the US. In economic news, the Reserve Bank of Australia kept interest rates steady at 3.85%, in an unexpected move. Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 was nearly unchanged at 8,590.70, while South Korea’s Kospi jumped 1.81% to 3,114.95. Japan’s Nikkei 225 gained 0.26% to finish at 39,688.81 and the Topix rose 0.17% to 2,816.54. In the mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index added 0.7% to 3,497.48, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index advanced 1.09% to 24,148.07. Wall Street Wavers Over Tariff Concerns Wall Street's major indexes finished mostly lower overnight as President Donald Trump's renewed tariff threats cast a shadow over trade negotiations with key partners. The Dow Jones Industrial Average slipped 0.37% to end at 44,240.76. The S&P 500 inched down 0.07%, ending at 6,225.52, while the Nasdaq Composite added 0.03% and closed at 20,418.46. The choppy trading on Wall Street came as traders seemed reluctant to make more significant moves amid lingering uncertainty about President Donald Trump's trade policies. Trump said there would be no extension beyond the Aug. 1 deadline, when countries are expected to begin paying tariffs, as his administration continues sending letters to foreign officials notifying them of the rates on imported goods. He also said he would impose a 50% tariff on copper imports to the US and threatened duties as high as 200% on pharmaceutical imports. Traders are waiting for further clarity, with many expecting the ultimate tariff rates to not be as strict as what Trump has threatened. A lack of major U.S. economic data may also have kept some traders on the sidelines ahead of the release of the minutes of the Federal Reserve's latest monetary policy meeting on late Wednesday. Corporate The state of Selangor’s aspiration to become a smart, competitive and a regional leader in green economy and food security could be well on its way with the development of an EduTechnology Park and Food Security Hub on Carey Island. Selangor Mentri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the project not only reflects the state government’s grand vision but also strengthens Carey Island’s position as an international hub for technology-driven development, education, and food security. The said project will be undertaken via a series of memoranda of understanding (MOU’s) between Permodalan Negeri Selangor Bhd (PNSB) who will spearhead the project, and three other entities –SD Guthrie Bhd, IJM Corp Bhdand Yayasan Selangor. According to Amirudin, Yayasan Selangor will be tasked with developing an international-standard campus to enhance educational access and build human capital inside the EduTechnology Park. (The Star).Comment:We maintain a neutral stance on this development. This marks the second strategic collaboration involving Guthrie’s land on the island, aimed at unlocking asset value, leveraging its strategic location, and diversifying revenue through its new industrial park vertical. While the JV structure may mitigate some cost and risk burdens, the project still requires significant upfront capital outlay for execution. Our estimates show that the 470 acres (~190 ha) site represents a small portion of SDG’s total land bank in Malaysia (less than 1%). Given the early-stage nature of the project and the limited financial disclosures provided, we are unable to assess its full earnings impact at this point. As such, we maintain our earnings forecast and reiterate our SELL rating on SDG, with an unchanged TP of RM4.94 /share, based on 20x CY26 earnings. Further updates from management will be key in assessing the project's viability and timeline for value realisation. Separately, we maintain a neutral stance on the MoU signed with IJM, as the project has yet to be officially awarded and is unlikely to deliver any meaningful earnings contribution in the near term given the absence of details on the project types and the jobscopes. Nevertheless, we view this initiative positively from an ESG standpoint, given its alignment with national priorities on food security and sustainable education. All things considered, we reiterate our earnings forecasts and Buy recommendation on the stock, with an unchanged target price of RM3.46, premised on a P/B multiple of 1.1x and incorporating a 3% ESG premium.
