KUALA LUMPUR (June 30): Foreign shareholdings in AMMB Holdings Bhd (KL:AMBANK) and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK) have reached new highs in recent months, according to the latest data from CIMB Securities. AMMB’s foreign shareholding, excluding strategic investors, rose to a recent peak of 33.5% in March, up from 31.2% in December 2024. RHB, meanwhile, saw its foreign stake climb to 20.5% in May — its highest level in over 15 years — from 20.1% in April. In contrast, foreign shareholdings in large-cap lenders, including CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB), Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK), and Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), showed signs of easing from recent peaks. CIMB’s foreign ownership inched up to 33.9% in May, compared with 33.7% in April, but remained below the recent high of 35.6% recorded in February. Still, it was higher than the 31.4% level seen in May 2024. Public Bank’s foreign stake slipped to 25.6% in May 2025, down from 25.9% in April and below the recent peak of 26.9% in December 2024. Maybank posted a modest rebound, with foreign shareholding rising to 19.48% in May from 19.20% in April. However, this remained below the recent high of 20.68% reached in September 2024. Among other mid-cap banks, foreign shareholdings declined slightly. Hong Leong Bank Bhd (KL:HLBANK) saw its foreign stake dip to 11.8% in March from 12.1% in December 2024, while Alliance Bank Malaysia Bhd's (KL:ABMB) foreign shareholding fell to 21.8% in May from 22.5% in April, well below its recent peak of 23.9% in August 2024. CIMB Securities maintained its 'neutral' call on the banking sector, citing upside risks such as lower credit costs, improved net interest margins, and bond-related gains in non-interest income. However, it flagged downside risks including rising funding costs, potential liquidity outflows, and deteriorating asset quality. The house also kept ‘buy’ calls on ABMB, Hong Leong Bank, Public Bank, and RHB Bank, supported by their dividend yields. It noted merger and acquisition potential in ABMB and earnings contributions from Hong Leong Bank’s Chinese associate — Bank of Chengdu Co Ltd — as additional catalysts. Source: TheEdge - 1 Jul 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (June 26): CIMB Securities expects the upcoming mandatory Employees Provident Fund (EPF) contributions for foreign workers, effective October 2025, to have a mildly negative impact on earnings, especially in labour-heavy sectors like plantations, gloves, technology, and construction. It said in a note on Thursday that earnings impact will likely start in late 2025 and be clearer in 2026. Under Budget 2025, employers and foreign workers must each contribute 2% of monthly wages to the EPF. Previously, these contributions were voluntary. The policy aims to reduce the gap in social protection between local and foreign workers. While the initial cost impact is manageable, any future hike in contribution rates could worsen the impact on earnings. CIMB in its note said the plantation sector would be the most affected, with many firms having not planned for the cost. It estimates a drop of 0.4% to 1.3% in financial year 2026 (FY2026) earnings for the plantation stocks it covers. For the glove sector, the cost is expected to add to already rising expenses amid weak demand. Labour makes up 9% to 10% of operating costs, limiting room to raise prices. The technology sector will also be affected, according to CIMB, especially electronics manufacturing services (EMS) and outsourced semiconductor assembly and test (OSAT) companies, because they rely heavily on foreign workers. Foreign workers make up 60% to 65% of the workforce for EMS, and between 20% and 25% for OSAT. CIMB estimates a 3% to 4% hit to OSAT earnings in FY2026. EMS players, meanwhile, may pass on some costs to customers. For the construction sector, CIMB expects EPF contributions to raise annual costs by RM200 million. The impact, however, is seen as manageable, with contractors expected to adjust future bids. CIMB said over time, it believes rising labour costs may drive companies to cut reliance on foreign labour and invest in automation. That said, CIMB said the EPF is expected to benefit from an additional RM2.4 billion annually, based on 2.5 million foreign workers, which could help support local market liquidity. CIMB maintained its FBM KLCI year-end target of 1,560 points, with its top stock picks including CelcomDigi Bhd (KL:CDB), Gamuda Bhd (KL:GAMUDA), Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK) and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK). Source: TheEdge - 27 Jun 2025

Loan growth has started to lose steam, with softer numbers from March 2025 compared to the strong momentum in late 2024. The slowdown is largely due to rising global uncertainties and a more cautious tone in both business and consumer sentiment. Many corporates have shifted to a “wait-and-see” approach, while consumer loan growth has also eased slightly from the 6.5% YoY seen in April 2024. Considering these developments, our overall loan growth forecast for 2025 has been revised down from 6.4% to 5.7% YoY. Net interest margins (NIM) are expected to remain stable, though mild compression is possible depending on policy rate movements, funding dynamics, and competition. While some banks are bracing for a potential OPR cut, most have not yet factored it into their official guidance. If a cut does happen, we estimate that sector earnings could dip by around 1.8%. However, Bank Negara Malaysia’s (BNM) recent move to lower the Statutory Reserve Requirement (SRR) from 2% to 1% should help offset the impact, injecting liquidity and potentially boosting the sector’s earnings by about 1.9%. Non-interest income (non-NII) will likely play a bigger role in supporting revenue, driven by trading and investment income, as well as steady growth in fee-based segments like bancassurance and wealth management. Non-NII is becoming more strategic as banks invest in partnerships and digital tools to diversify their revenue streams and enhance returns. The stability in loan demand also provides a solid base for maintaining fee income. Banks remain focused on managing costs tightly while continuing to invest in digital transformation. Although IT spending is expected to rise as part of long-term strategic priorities, banks are balancing this with broader cost control initiatives. As a result, the sector’s cost-to-income ratio is projected to stay at around 46% in 2025, with steady income growth providing a cushion against rising expenses. Asset quality remains resilient, supported by prudent risk management despite pockets of sector-specific stress. The system-wide gross impaired loans ratio has held steady at around 1.43%, and credit costs are expected to decline further. While banks have started releasing some management overlays, they continue to hold substantial provision buffers as a safeguard. With solid fundamentals, strong capital positions, and attractive valuations, the KL Finance Index (KLFI) continues to outperform the FBM KLCI. We maintain our OVERWEIGHT stance on the sector, given its resilience, earnings visibility, and capacity to support credit flows and economic stability. Our stock picks for the sector are CIMB Group Holdings Berhad (TP: RM8.86, ★★★★), Hong Leong Bank Berhad (TP: RM23.59, ★★★★), and Public Bank Berhad (TP: RM5.15, ★★★★). Loans and Advances Business Loans Loan Growth Momentum Loses Steam Amid Global Uncertainty Loan growth has started to ease, coming in below expectations and marking a break from the stronger momentum seen in the second half of 2024. To recap, loan activity had been picking up steadily during that period, with a notable rebound in business loans as confidence and demand improved. However, this momentum did not to carry through into March and the 2Q of 2025, dampened by rising global uncertainty following Donald Trump's return to the U.S. presidency and renewed volatility stemming from trade and tariff tensions. Since March 2025, business sentiment has turned cautious, with many companies adopting a "wait-and-see" approach. This shift has begun to show in Bank Negara Malaysia's (BNM) monthly data. According to BNM, business loan growth moderated to 4.1% YoY in April 2025, down from a stronger 5.4% YoY expansion in April 2024. Loans for working capital also grew at a slower pace of 4.1% YoY (April 2024: 5.3%), reflecting softer overall business activity. On a sector level, loans to the manufacturing sector — which account for roughly 15% of total loans — remained muted, registering a 4.4% YoY growth in April 2025. This aligns with Malaysia's recent Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) readings, which suggest that demand conditions in the manufacturing sector remained lacklustre during the month. Both production and new orders continued to weaken, weighed down by subdued domestic and external demand. Manufacturers reported ongoing declines in new business inflows, and with external markets still showing limited signs of recovery, production volumes may face further pressure. In response, many firms are expected to scale back purchasing activity and continue drawing down on existing raw materials and finished goods inventory to manage costs more effectively Construction Loan Growth Weakens, But Structural Tailwinds Remain The construction sector, which we had anticipated to perform more strongly, has recorded declining loan growth for five consecutive months. The latest figures from April 2025 show a 7.6% YoY contraction. Construction loans comprise around 11% of total business loans, making this a notable drag on overall lending momentum. That said, not all segments are slowing. Earlier concerns over the US restrictions on AI chip exports — which raised fears of a broader tech slowdown and a potential pullback in data centre investments appear overstated as construction activity related to data centres remains resilient. Developers continue to tap into rising demand driven by the artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and Internet of Things (IoT) sectors, with several new data centre projects already in the pipeline. Beyond the tech-driven demand, construction activity is also expected to be supported by government-led initiatives, particularly flood mitigation. At the same time, rising demand for housing and industrial properties, along with strategic projects such as the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ), the Special Financial Zone (SFZ) in Forest City, and urbanisation efforts in areas like Seberang Perai and Batu Kawan, are contributing to a solid project pipeline. These developments are expected to underpin the medium-term growth of the construction and non-residential property sectors. Pockets of Business Loan Growth Remain, But Broader Momentum Softens Despite the recent moderation in overall business loan growth, we continue to see selective areas of opportunity. Notably, loans in the Electricity, Gas, Steam & Air Conditioning Supply sector surged by a strong 24.5% YoY in April 2025. This sharp increase aligns with Malaysia’s push toward clean energy under the National Energy Transition Roadmap (NETR), which estimates that up to RM1.85 trillion in financing will be required to support the country’s energy transition efforts by 2050. With this backdrop, we believe the outlook remains positive for financing opportunities in renewable power generation, green mobility, and emerging technologies such as hydrogen and carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS). On the other hand, several industries continued to report weaker lending activity. Loans to the Education, Health & Others and Agriculture, Forestry & Fishing sectors declined by 7.1% and 8.8% YoY, respectively, while Mining & Quarrying fell by 3.8% YoY in April. Most other sectors, however, still posted positive YoY growth. Leading the gains were Information & Communication (+13.0% YoY), Water Supply, Sewerage & Waste Management (+10.3% YoY), Finance, Insurance & Business Activities (+10.0% YoY), followed by Accommodation & Food Services (+7.6% YoY), Transportation & Storage (+7.6% YoY), Manufacturing (+4.4% YoY), and Wholesale & Retail Trade (+3.4% YoY). Still, the YTD business loan growth remains below expectations, prompting us to revise our full-year business loan growth forecast for 2025, down from 5.5% to 4.9%. Adding to the cautious outlook, the latest RAM Business Confidence Index (BCI) for 1Q 2025 showed a notable deterioration in business sentiment. The index fell to 41.1 from 54.5 in the previous quarter — slipping back into pessimistic territory after four straight quarters of optimism. According to RAM, the weaker outlook reflects growing concerns over escalating global risks, particularly disruptions to global trade flows. These are expected to directly hit export sales and indirectly weigh on domestic business activity. Consumer Loans Consumer Loan Growth Moderates Amid Rising Cost Pressures Consumer loans, which comprise nearly 60% of total loans, continued to expand in April 2025, increasing 5.8% YoY and 0.4% MoM. However, this marked a slight moderation compared to the 6.5% YoY growth recorded in April 2024. Residential mortgages — accounting for around 65% of total consumer loans — remained the key driver, although growth slowed to 6.5% YoY from 7.5% a year earlier. Meanwhile, loans for purchasing passenger cars rose 7.2% YoY, easing from the stronger 10.1% growth recorded last year. Credit card lending and personal loans also grew slower, up 7.9% YoY (April 2024: +8.9%) and 3.3% YoY (April 2024: +5.7%), respectively. Loans for the purchase of securities remained in contraction, declining 2.8% YoY — though this was a narrower drop compared to -10.3% in March. Despite relatively healthy growth, YTD consumer loan expansion is also trailing our earlier forecast of 6.7% for 2025. While Malaysia’s Consumer Confidence Index rose by 4% in January 2025 — buoyed by improving sentiment around job security, future investment plans, and purchasing power — there are several headwinds. These include the rising cost of living due to subsidy rationalisation, as well as external pressures from ongoing trade tensions and geopolitical instability. These factors may prompt consumers to become more cautious in the second half of the year, potentially slowing borrowing activity. As a result, we revised our full-year consumer loan growth forecast lower to 6.3%. Key Consumer Loan Segments to Moderate, But Underlying Demand Remains Supportive We project residential mortgages to grow at a slightly softer pace of 6.9% YoY in 2025 — down from our earlier forecast of 7.4% YoY. Nonetheless, the outlook remains broadly optimistic, supported by resilient domestic demand, a stable labour market, accommodative monetary policy, and ongoing government efforts to encourage homeownership. We continue to expect the property sector to see stronger momentum this year, with developers under our coverage projected to post around 4% growth in property sales. Hire purchase (HP) loans for passenger cars — accounting for roughly 17% of total consumer loans — are also expected to grow more modestly, at 7.3% YoY in 2025 vs 8.5% in 2024. This moderation is largely due to a high base effect following several years of strong growth in total industry volume (TIV). For 2025, we anticipate the automotive sector will begin to normalise, with vehicle sales forecasted to decline to 700,000 units (-14.3% YoY) as demand settles after prolonged elevated activity. The sizeable backlog of vehicle orders, which had previously supported growth, has mostly cleared. Despite that, HP loans will remain a key contributor to consumer loan growth. We continue to see sustained demand for certain automotive brands, supported by the rollout of new models, facelifts, and variant expansions — helping to partially offset the overall sector slowdown. Meanwhile, we now expect credit card loans to grow by 5.7% YoY and personal loans to rise by 4.0% YoY. Loan Growth Outlook Trimmed Amid Cautious Business Sentiment Bringing everything together, we have revised our overall loan growth forecast for 2025 downwards from 6.4% YoY to 5.7% YoY, reflecting more tempered expectations for both consumer and business loans. In line with this, we have also lowered our loan growth projections for all banks under coverage from 6.7% to 6.1%, as most financial institutions balance strategic caution and selective expansion. To recap the latest 1QCY25 results,Alliance Bankstood out with an impressive loan growth of 12% YoY — twice the industry pace — supported by strong momentum in SME lending (+12% YoY), consumer loans (+13% YoY) and targeted growth in economic corridors such as Penang (+20% YoY) and Sarawak (+17% YoY).Affin Bankalso delivered a solid performance, with gross loans rising 7.1% YoY, led by substantial gains in community banking (+10%) and enterprise banking (+7.5%). In contrast, other financial institutions have guided for more moderate growth in the months ahead. Banks cited a slowdown in loan demand, particularly in the commercial and corporate segments, as businesses delay investment decisions amid ongoing global tariff uncertainties. For instance,RHB Bankhas adopted a more cautious outlook, revising its 2025 loan growth guidance to 5–6% (from 6–7%), citing expectations of softer GDP growth. Similarly,AMMBandPublic Bankare both targeting mid-single-digit loan growth of around 5–6%, whileCIMBcontinues to emphasise disciplined, responsible asset expansion, particularly in the consumer and commercial segments. That said, select high-yield segments continue to see vigorous competitive activity.Public BankandMaybankflagged heightened competition in the mortgage and SME space. ForAffin Bank, mortgage and HP loans remain key drivers, as the bank targets growth in national car financing to improve credit diversification.AMMBis also seeing traction in both HP and RSME (Retail SME) portfolios. Meanwhile,Hong Leong Bankaims to further grow its HP portfolio, with plans to have 10% of its auto loan origination fully digital by the second half of the year. Net Interest Margin NIM Is Likely to Stay Stable to Slightly Lower Amid Policy and Competitive Headwinds Net interest margins (NIMs) should remain broadly stable or face mild compression across the banking sector, shaped by evolving policy rate expectations, funding mix dynamics, and ongoing competitive pressures. While most banks have acknowledged the possibility of an Overnight Policy Rate (OPR) cut later this year, they have yet to factor it into their official NIM guidance. Nonetheless, we estimate that should an OPR cut materialise, it could shave approximately 1.8% off sector earnings. Beyond potential monetary easing, NIM compression also reflects lingering competitive pressure, particularly on the funding side. While the battle for deposits was significantly more intense last year, the situation has eased somewhat in recent months. According to BNM’s April 2025 data, the industry’s average deposit rates — which peaked in 1QCY2024 — have since moderated and stabilised at around 2.69% for 12-month fixed deposits (FDs). The initial disruption caused by aggressive deposit campaigns from digital banks has also begun to normalise. For example,CIMBhas successfully lowered its campaign deposit rates by 10–20 bps, supported by the group’s strong capital and liquidity positions. While selective promotional FD campaigns are still in the market, they are now more contained, with most 12-month FD rates ranging between 3.80% and 4.10% as of May 2025. On the asset side, some banks are seeing rising price competition, particularly in business loans and the mortgage segment. This is reflected in the overall average lending rate, which has eased to 4.92% in April 2025, down from 4.97% in March and 5.36% a year ago. However, with deposit competition cooling, sector-wide NIMs have stabilised at around 2%. Liquidity and Capital Buffers Remain Healthy; SRR Cut Provides Added Relief The banking system’s liquidity position remains solid, with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) rising slightly to around 156% in April 2025, up slightly from 152% a year ago. The loan-to-fund ratio also climbed to 83.3% (April 2024: 82.0%), reflecting sufficient liquidity buffers. On the capital front, banks continue to maintain strong positions, with a Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 14.3% and a Total Capital Ratio of 17.7%, well above regulatory minimums. Helping cushion the impact of any potential OPR cut and margin compression, BNM’s decision last month to reduce the Statutory Reserve Requirement (SRR) from 2% to 1% has injected a welcome liquidity boost into the system. As highlighted in our earlier report, we view this move as a net positive for the sector. The estimated RM19bn liquidity release provides banks with added balance sheet flexibility — supporting loan growth and helping manage funding costs. Our projections suggest the improved liquidity could lift sector earnings by approximately 1.9%.AMMBandRHB Bank, for example, are set to receive around RM1.25bn and RM1.7bn, respectively, from this liquidity release. Banks Refining Asset Strategies; CASA Remains a Key Focus Despite Slowing Growth Amid margin pressures and evolving rate dynamics, banks are actively refining their asset and liability strategies to optimise funding costs and strengthen liquidity profiles. A key area of focus has been on improving the deposit mix — particularly in growing low-cost Current and Savings Account (CASA) deposits. Affin Bankstands out with a strong 36.2% YoY CASA growth in 1QCY25, surpassing its CASA mix target of 32%, largely supported by its push in community and enterprise banking.Alliance Bankalso posted a solid 13.1% YoY increase in demand deposits, bolstered by inflows from government accounts and stronger traction in emerging growth corridors such as Penang and Sarawak. Meanwhile,Maybankis actively rebalancing its deposit structure by reducing reliance on short-term wholesale money market deposits and shifting towards more stable, cost-efficient fixed deposits (FDs).AMMB, on the other hand, expects its margins to hold relatively steady even in the event of an OPR cut, thanks to ongoing adjustments in asset mix and strategic funding management — including a bond portfolio positioned to benefit from a declining rate environment. Still, on a system-wide level, CASA growth has shown signs of softening. As of April 2025, total CASA grew by 4.8% YoY (+0.2% MoM), moderating from 6.1% growth a year ago. That said, there are still opportunities for banks to strengthen CASA traction. For instance,Affin Bankanticipates further CASA uplift from the Sarawak government payroll mandate and the rollout of its new mobile banking app, AffinAlwaysX.RHB Bankis also tapping into untapped segments through initiatives like its RHB MySiswa Debit Card-i programme — a collaboration with the Ministry of Higher Education — which has onboarded nearly 500,000 university students to date, driving incremental CASA growth while promoting financial literacy. Non-Interest Income Non-interest Income Growth to Moderate, But the Outlook Remains Positive For CY25 and CY26, we project non-interest income (non-NII) for the banks under our coverage to grow by 8.7% and 9.2%, respectively. While these are healthy rates, they mark a moderation from the strong 15.7% YoY jump in CY2024. To recap, the sharp rise in 2024 was largely underpinned by robust fee income, solid trading and investment gains, and favourable forex movements amid heightened market volatility. Looking ahead, we believe several drivers will continue to support non-NII growth in 2025 and beyond. Continued global market uncertainty — particularly around the pace of U.S. Fed rate cuts and currency movements — will likely sustain opportunities for trading and investment income. On the fee income side, which rose by 8.9% YoY in 2024, we expect a degree of stability underpinned by steady loan demand and growing momentum in the Bancassurance and Wealth Management segments. Encouragingly, 1QCY25 results already show positive trends:Hong Leong Bankled the pack with a 10.1% YoY rise in fee income, followed byCIMB(+6.7%) andAlliance Bank(+5.1%). Conversely, capital market activities have taken a step back. Although the prevailing stable interest rate environment offers a supportive backdrop for mergers, acquisitions, and other corporate exercises, heightened market volatility has led some companies to delay their IPO plans or revise offer prices downward. These decisions reflect growing concerns over weaker investor sentiment, reduced valuations, and a cautious market outlook. Nonetheless, opportunities remain. Current geopolitical tensions may spur increased focus on businesses oriented towards domestic and intra-Asia trade. We believe sectors likely to benefit from this shift include environmental and renewable energy assets, as well as digital infrastructure such as telecommunications towers and data centres. Despite softer equity activity, data from BNM underscores the resilience of Malaysia’s capital markets. In the first four months of 2025, net funds raised by the private sector reached RM46.2bn (excluding redemptions), up from RM34.2bn in the same period last year. This comprised RM1.4bn from new equity and warrant issuances (slightly below RM1.7bn in YTD 2024) and RM44.8bn from new debt securities (versus RM32.5bn previously). Non-NII remains a key lever for revenue diversification and ROE uplift We view non-NII as a critical driver for revenue diversification and ROE enhancement. Banks are increasingly turning to strategic partnerships and digital investments to strengthen this pillar. We believe that one promising development isHong Leong Bank’s new strategic alliance with Lombard Odier to scale its wealth management proposition. Through this collaboration, HLB’s private banking clients will gain access to global CIO insights, advanced investment technology, and tailored product solutions — all geared toward building intergenerational wealth in a costefficient manner. Affin Bankis also making targeted investments, including new credit card and trade finance systems, to drive growth in higher-margin credit card business and fee-based income. Its recent partnership with MUFG is expected to open up opportunities in FX funding and create broader synergy benefits. Elsewhere,Alliance Bankis focusing on growing fee and treasury income through FX sales, trade fees, and wealth management offerings.Public Bankis intensifying cross-selling efforts with LPI Capital to boost bancassurance income, with a more meaningful uplift expected from FY26 onward.RHB, meanwhile, sees continued upside from its wealth and asset management businesses, bancassurance, and transactional banking segments. Operating Expenses Tightening Grip on Operating Expenses Banks are expected to maintain a tighter grip on overhead expenses, moving into CY25 and CY26, with operating expenses projected to rise by around 6.6% in both years. This comes after a 7.9% YoY increase recorded in CY24, largely driven by higher spending on personnel (+9.3% YoY), marketing (+15.3% YoY), and establishment costs (+12.7% YoY). The increase was partially offset by a slight contraction in administrative and general expenses, which declined by 0.5% YoY. Operational efficiency remains a key focus, with banks continuing to balance cost control measures alongside long-term investments in technology and automation. While spending on IT infrastructure is expected to remain a structural cost driver, these investments are becoming increasingly essential as banks navigate a more complex digital landscape. Rising cyber threats, evolving data privacy regulations, and shifting customer expectations are pushing financial institutions to enhance their digital capabilities. The ongoing transition toward digital banking has made cybersecurity and system resilience critical components of operational stability. At the same time, demand for more sophisticated and convenient banking services is accelerating. According to BNM, e-payment adoption — including e-money and mobile banking — continues to grow, underpinned by increasing consumer awareness, education, and the availability of supporting infrastructure. In this environment, continued investment in digital channels is necessary not only to sustain service quality and operational efficiency but also to drive incremental revenue. We see opportunities for banks to deepen customer relationships through enhanced digital product offerings, potentially improving product holding ratios, fee-based income, and ROE. Recent bank briefings further highlight this strategic pivot.AMMBhas reaffirmed its focus on operational excellence through greater use of automation and digital tools.Affin Bankis channelling resources into new credit card and trade finance systems to improve margins and expand its fee income base. Meanwhile,Hong LeongBank’spartnership with WeBank aims to streamline digital processes, particularly within auto financing and SME lending.Alliance Bank, on the other hand, is leveraging AI-driven solutions to support ESG reporting for newly listed companies. Technology-driven Investments to Keep Costs Elevated While these technology-driven investments are expected to keep operating costs elevated in the near term—exemplified byMaybank’sguidance under its M25+ strategic roadmap— they are crucial for building long-term operational resilience and efficiency.Alliance Bank, for instance, is guiding for a stable cost-to-income (CTI) ratio of 48% in FY26 despite a strong revenue outlook, reflecting its continued investments in both talent and technology. Notably,Hong Leong Bankhas demonstrated how technology-led efficiency gains can translate into superior cost management; the bank reported a CTI ratio of just 38.8%—the lowest among peers—attributed to disciplined cost controls and AI-driven process optimisation. Looking ahead, we expect banks to continue managing personnel costs by improving staff productivity rather than expanding headcount. Sector employment data points to a more subdued hiring environment, with job vacancies and total employment in the financial services sector declining by 0.4% and 1.2% YoY, respectively. Beyond human capital, we also see scope for banks to revisit the optimal size and distribution of their physical branch networks. With regional economic growth gaining momentum in markets such as Penang, Sabah, Sarawak, and Johor, several banks should reassess their physical presence to better align with growth opportunities. As a reference, there are currently 240 commercial bank branches in East Malaysia, compared to 144 each in Penang and Perak, 235 in Johor, 271 in Wilayah Persekutuan, and 402 in Selangor. Given the development potential in Sabah and Sarawak, resource reallocation toward these regions could enhance customer reach and support inclusive banking growth strategies. Despite the anticipated rise in operating expenses, we expect the banking sector’s cost-toincome (CTI) ratio to remain broadly stable, averaging around 46.0% in CY25 and 46.3% in CY26 — only marginally higher than the 45.8% recorded in CY24. This reflects our view that steady income growth will provide a sufficient buffer against elevated cost pressures, allowing banks to preserve overall efficiency. In our view, ongoing efforts to enhance productivity through digitalisation and cost optimisation initiatives will continue to play a critical role in containing structural cost escalation and supporting sustainable profitability across the sector. Asset Quality Asset Quality Trends Overall, asset quality across the banking sector remains resilient, supported by proactive risk management practices despite emerging sector-specific headwinds. Total impaired loans in the system continued to trend lower, underpinned by reductions in both consumer and business segments. However, certain banks did observe mild upticks in impairments within specific consumer portfolios — particularly in mortgages, hire purchases, and unsecured loans. That said, the increases were not material, and the system-wide gross impaired loans (GIL) ratio held steady at around 1.43%, supported by consumer and business GIL ratios of 1.09% and 4.33%, respectively. Among banks under our coverage, asset quality also showed continued YoY improvement. The average GIL ratio improved to 1.54% in 1QCY25, down from 1.67% in the prior year — closely tracking the broader industry trend. New impaired loan formations, however, registered a modest yearly and sequential uptick, as observed in 1Q 2025, attributed to pockets of stress in the consumer segment. Nevertheless, most banks reported stable and low GIL ratios in their latest results.Public BankandHong Leong Bank, in particular, continued to lead in asset quality, posting GIL ratios of 0.53% and 0.57%, respectively. Nonetheless, several banks flagged emerging areas of concern that are being closely monitored. For example,AffinBankhighlighted a rise in mortgage impairments linked to cost-of-living pressures, while its Enterprise Banking Stage 2 loans remained elevated due to delayed payments from buyers, weaker export activity, and tighter liquidity conditions. Alliance Bank also pointed out some signs of stress in its unsecured lending portfolio. On the business side, exposure to external tariffs has been identified as a potential risk, though banks generally view their direct exposure to the US — estimated at 3–4% of total loans — as manageable. Still, there is growing attention on second-order effects, such as reduced investor flows or delays in corporate capital expenditure. In response, banks have been proactively engaging with clients to assess risks and implement early intervention strategies where needed. Improvements in Provisions and Credit Charges Provisioning trends across the banking sector continue to improve, supported by reductions in Stage 2 and Stage 3 Lifetime Expected Credit Losses (ECL), according to BNM. This trend is also evident among banks under our coverage, with 1Q provisions showing YoY improvement. On a quarterly basis, provisions also improved, driven notably byHong Leong Bank’s net allowance writeback of RM387mn following an MOA release of RM399mn. Looking ahead, we expect the improvement in provisions to continue, underpinned by more optimistic credit cost guidance from several banks. We project gross credit costs to ease further to 27.9 bps in CY2025 and 26.3 bps in CY2026.CIMB, for example, has revised its credit cost guidance lower from 30–40 bps to 25–35 bps, whileAMMBexpects net credit costs to remain below 30 bps in FY26. The only notable exception isRHB, which anticipates a slight uptick in credit costs following downward revisions to GDP forecasts. Nevertheless, RHB has intensified its domestic recovery efforts to mitigate the impact. That said, while the overall outlook on credit costs remains optimistic, there are still some downside risks, particularly from potential Macroeconomic Variables (MEV) adjustments if macro data turns out weaker-than-expected. Despite lower provisioning trends, banks also continue to maintain sizeable management overlays as precautionary buffers against macroeconomic uncertainties. As of the latest data,Maybankholds the largest overlay at RM1.8bn, followed byPublic Bank(RM1.2bn),AMMB(RM439mn), andRHB(RM317mn). While there have been some selective writebacks, we estimate that the total accumulated management overlays across banks under our coverage still stand at approximately RM6.0bn, down from RM7.8bn as of June 2024. Going forward, we anticipate banks will continue to reallocate or partially unwind these overlays, depending on how economic conditions evolve in 2025. That said, preserving a healthy loan loss coverage (LLC) ratio remains a key focus. We note that the average LLC for banks under coverage has declined to 102.5% as of the latest quarter from 113.3% in 1QCY24. Among those with LLC ratios below 100% areAMMB, RHB Bank, Hong Leong Bank,andAffin Bank, highlighting the need for continued prudence in managing provisioning buffers. Capital Adequate Buffers and Strong Capital Ratios The Malaysian banking sector continues demonstrating healthy capital positions, with buffers well above regulatory thresholds, underscoring the system’s resilience and capacity to support future growth. As of April 2025, the industry’s Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio stood at 14.3%, while the Total Capital Ratio was 17.7%. Liquidity also remains ample, with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) at a robust 156%, providing further assurance amid a backdrop of external uncertainties. All banks under our coverage are expected to maintain sound capital buffers, supported by prudent balance sheet management and conservative provisioning strategies. Among recent developments,Alliance Bankannounced a proposed RM600mn renounceable rights issue in March 2025 to strengthen its capital position. The issue, priced at RM3.33 per rights share on the basis of two rights shares for every 17 existing shares, received overwhelming shareholder approval with 99.9% votes in favour at its EGM. The move is expected to raise the group’s CET1 ratio by 1.1 percentage points to 13.5%, narrowing the gap with the industry average. Similarly,Affin Bankopted to issue bonus shares in lieu of cash dividends, signalling its focus on retaining capital to support growth initiatives. Dividends and Payout Policies Despite macroeconomic headwinds, most banks appear well-positioned to sustain their dividend policies, supported by healthy earnings generation and capital strength.AMMB, for instance, has raised its dividend payout commitment to 50% under its new strategic plan—up from the previous 40%—and targets to sustain this into FY26, given its improved capital trajectory. Despite its ongoing rights issue,Alliance Bankhas maintained its dividend payout guidance of 40–50%, reflecting confidence in its capital position post-issuance.CIMBcontinues to adhere to its 40–60% dividend policy and has consistently delivered a 55% payout in recent years. The group has also expressed openness to returning additional capital to shareholders through special dividends, depending on its capital position and growth needs. Valuation and Recommendation Attractive Valuations Persist Amid Outperformance The KLFIN Index has continued to outperform the broader FBM KLCI, in line with our overweight stance on the banking sector. Despite this outperformance, we believe the sector remains fundamentally attractive from a valuation perspective. The sector currently trades at a forward FY25 P/B ratio of 0.97x, supported by an industry ROE of approximately 10.1% and an average dividend yield of 5.3%. Most banks—excluding Affin Bank—are trading at or below one standard deviation below their 10-year P/BV mean, reinforcing the view that valuations remain undemanding and offer upside potential. Stabilising Foreign Shareholding Trends Following a marginal dip in foreign shareholding during 1Q 2025, most banks have reported a recovery in foreign investor interest through April and May. That said, foreign shareholding levels remain below historical peaks, indicating room for further inflows. Maybank and CIMB remain the key proxies for global investors seeking exposure to Malaysia's financial sector and broader macroeconomic fundamentals.Maybank'sforeign shareholding rose to 19.5% in May 2025 from 19.2% in April, whileCIMBsaw its foreign ownership peak at 36.1% in late 2024 before easing slightly to 33.9% as of May 2025.Public Bankalso experienced a meaningful rise in foreign shareholding from 19.0% to 26.9% by the end of 2024 before dipping to 25.6% in May 2025. Foreign Rotation Into Mid-cap Banks We also observe a slight shift in foreign investor positioning, with increasing interest in midsized banks.RHB Bank, in particular, has seen a steady rise in foreign shareholding — from 13.97% in May 2024 to 20.45% — reflecting renewed investor confidence.AMMBhas also attracted consistent foreign inflows, with shareholding levels increasing from 20% in December 2023 to 33.5% in March 2025. This momentum is supported by its earnings recovery, strengthened capital buffers post-1MDB resolution, and improving dividend track record However, foreign shareholding inHong Leong Bankhas tapered recently, potentially due to concerns around softer contributions from its associate, Bank of Chengdu. Smaller banks likeAlliance BankandAffin Bankhave also reported a slight decline in foreign interest. For Alliance Bank, earlier foreign interest may have been driven by market speculation around a potential M&A with DBS Bank, while Affin Bank's earlier momentum may have been linked to optimism surrounding its new strategic shareholder — the Sarawak state government — and the anticipated uplift in earnings. Earnings Growth Outlook Remains Resilient for 2025 Despite lingering macroeconomic uncertainties, the earnings outlook for the Malaysian banking sector in 2025 remains constructive. Sector earnings are projected to expand by 4.6% to RM37.3bn, underpinned by sound fundamentals and continued operational resilience. Loan growth is expected to accelerate by 5.7%, supported by healthy demand across both retail and business segments. With margins likely to stay relatively stable, NII is forecasted to rise by 4.3%. Islamic banking contributions are also set to see healthy momentum, with growth anticipated at 7.0%—driven by deeper market penetration and rising customer adoption of Shariahcompliant offerings. Non-NII is poised for an 8.7% uplift, reflecting stronger fee income traction and broader diversification across bancassurance, wealth, and digital services. Asset quality is projected to remain broadly stable, with the sector's credit cost expected to ease slightly to 27.9 bps, further cushioning profitability. Meanwhile, robust capital and liquidity buffers position banks to pursue growth opportunities and withstand potential external shocks. Overall, we maintain a positive stance on the sector's earnings trajectory heading into 2025. With these positive indicators, we maintain anOVERWEIGHTstance on the sector. Our top recommendations for the sector are: 1) CIMB, in our view, remains a compelling stock pick, supported by its new Forward30 Strategic Plan, which emphasises capital reallocation and optimisation to strengthen its ASEAN footprint amid a volatile macro landscape. Asset quality continues to exceed expectations, with loan loss charges improving to 26 bps in 1QFY25 and management lowering credit cost guidance to 25–35 bps while maintaining prudent provisioning through additional overlays. Foreign investor interest remains stable, with shareholding rising to 33.9%, aided by CIMB’s strong ESG credentials and improving financial performance. Backed by a solid CET1 ratio of 14.6%, CIMB has delivered special dividends for two consecutive years and maintains a sustainable 40–50% dividend payout policy. Trading at 1.0x FY25 P/B— around its 10-year average and below peers—CIMB offers an attractive valuation upside. 2) Public Bank, because earnings remain heavily anchored in its domestic, consumercentric operations, retail and hire purchase segments contribute 55% to group PBT and 94% of overall profits derived locally. The bank’s strategic focus on domestic retail and SME lending aligns well with expectations of Malaysia-led macro growth in 2025. PBB also benefits from sustained unit trust income amid global market volatility. Asset quality remains a standout, with a low impaired loan ratio of 0.5%, high loan loss coverage at 159.9%, and a strong RM1.2bn management overlay. Operational efficiency is a key strength, reflected in a lean 35% CTI ratio vs peers’ 43.6%. Currently trading near -1 SD of its 10-year P/B mean, PBB offers a compelling valuation upside, especially with improving foreign investor interest— foreign shareholding rose significantly in 2024 before a slight pullback in 2025, leaving room for re-entry supported by its robust fundamentals and high ROE. 3) Hong Leong Bankas it continues to outperform peers, particularly in consumer and SME loans, with 3Q FY25 loan growth reaching 7.2%, surpassing industry averages and management's guidance. This fuelled solid topline growth, including a 34.1% YoY rise in non-NII. Core pre-BOCD profit rose 24.2% YoY in 9MFY25, supported by healthy contributions from wealth management, global markets, and SME banking. Overseas, HLBB posted robust loan growth in Singapore and Vietnam. Asset quality remains exceptional, with a low GIL ratio of 0.57%, though LLC declined due to a RM399mn MOA release. Strategic partnerships with Lombard Odier and WeBank are expected to further elevate HLBB's wealth and digital banking propositions. Potential Risks and Challenges While the outlook for the banking sector remains broadly positive, there are several risks worth monitoring. A key concern is the potential deterioration in asset quality should external shocks emerge, especially in a still-fragile global economic environment. Softer contributions from regional operations may also materialise if the ringgit strengthens further, eroding foreign-sourced earnings. On the domestic front, rising competition in loan pricing could lead to some margin compression, while sustained investments in technology and talent may keep overhead costs elevated in the near term. Additionally, while banks remain committed to dividend payouts, these could come under pressure as the sector prepares for the potential capital impact from the implementation of Basel 3.5. That said, we believe the sector is still well-supported by solid fundamentals, with stable asset quality, healthy capital buffers, and decent income growth cushioning against these risks. Source: TA Research - 26 Jun 2025

Executive Summary Israel's attack on Iran and the United States’ direct involvement in the conflict are significant impediments to market recovery. These untimely actions are particularly concerning as the world anticipates easing tariff impacts and a financial market recovery in the second half of the year following successful trade negotiations. The situation could worsen if it provokes severe retaliation from Iran and its supporters. An amicable resolution to the US's reciprocal tariffs would have mitigated a major market dampener that fuelled concerns about a potential US recession and a severe global economic slowdown. Our base case FBMKLCI target of 1,660, utilising a CY26 PER of 14.3x (which is -0.7 standard deviation from the long-term mean of 16x), assumes that the current reduced tariffs will remain in place for most nations, including Malaysia's 10%, the deadline for these tariffs will likely be extended if a comprehensive deal cannot be finalized on time, and the IsraelIran conflict is contained and short-lived. We view the ongoing consolidation following a 200-point surge between April and May as a healthy phase for the benchmark index to advance once clarity emerges post the truce period in the 3Q25. This outlook acknowledges the remote possibility of key trading nations securing an equally beneficial deal with the US but assumes that the concessions granted should suffice to maintain momentum without significantly hindering global economic growth. In this context, OPEC+'s initiative to increase output was anticipated to exert downward pressure on crude oil prices and maintain a tight lid on inflation, thereby softening the US Federal Reserve's stringent monetary stance. However, these expectations are now tempered by the Israel-Iran war. Consequently, we introduce a worst-case target of 1,580, applying a minus one standard deviation, should tariff negotiations fall short of expectations and the conflict between the arch-rivals persists throughout the year with minimal impact on regional stability and global security. Source:TA Research- 25 Jun 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (June 23): Malaysian stocks rebounded from early losses on Monday, bucking a broad decline in Asian markets, thanks to gains of heavyweight banks while energy stocks rallied amid a surge in oil prices. The benchmark FBM KLCI opened nearly 10 points lower, before falling to as low as 1,488.89 points, its lowest since April 22. The index then rebounded to a high of 1,509.77 points before pausing for midday break at 1,509.55 points, marking a 0.5% gain from Friday. The index closed at 1,516.61 points, marking a 0.92% gain for the day. Energy and financial services were the only two sectoral indices in the green, standing out amid a sea of red across Bursa Malaysia. Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK) led gains, climbing 4.99% to RM4.42, while CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB) rose 2.26% to RM6.80. Investors should brace for further downside in the stock market if the Iran-Israel military conflict escalates into a prolonged war, MIDF Research said. There are still short-term trading opportunities in energy stocks, even as investors should stay defensive for the medium term amid the ongoing uncertainty, the house said. Hibiscus Petroleum Bhd (KL:HIBISCS) climbed as much as 7% to RM1.83, while Hengyuan Refining Company Bhd (KL:HENGYUAN) rose nearly 4% to RM1.92. The Bursa Malaysia Energy Index, which tracks 31 stocks in the sector, was up over 1%. Brent, the global benchmark for crude oil, is now trading at US$77.57 (RM333.16) per barrel, after rising more than 30% from this year’s low in May. Analysts have been warning that any disruption in the Strait of Hormuz — through which about 20% of global oil supply passes — could trigger another surge in oil prices. In the near term, Brent crude price could hit US$85 per barrel, driven by supply-side risks, said CIMB Securities. A supply disruption of 500,000 barrels of oil per day could result in a price increase of US$10 per barrel, based on historical events, the house noted. Dialog Group Bhd (KL:DIALOG), in particular, is expected to benefit from rising oil prices due to its upstream assets that contributed 35% to its FY2024 core net profit, CIMB noted. Source: TheEdge - 24 Jun 2025

Malaysia equities struggled on risk-off sentiment Lingering policy ambiguity has been a key overhang for Malaysian equities. The KLCI has shed 8.6% YTD, making it the second worst performing market in SEA. Small caps have not been spared, with the FBM Small Cap index declining 16.0% YTD amid subdued liquidity and a rotation into defensive large-cap names. The Malaysian equity market recorded US$2.5bn net foreign outflows in 1H25, marking the most significant exodus since 2018-19. Tariff concerns will begin to abate The US tariff may trigger another bout of volatility in 3Q25 as markets digest the decision and its post-90-day implications. Our base case assumes a more constructive resolution, with Malaysia likely to take a pragmatic stance in exchange for tariff relief, which could support a market recovery in 4Q25. A de-escalation in trade tensions could see rotation of foreign capital back into US assets, leading to a mild depreciation of the Ringgit toward RM4.30:US$1 by year-end. Malaysia's inflation is expected to stay low at 2% in 2025E, supporting BNM's decision to maintain OPR at 3%. Our 2025E GDP forecast is revised down to 4.3%. Overweight on 8 key sectors We maintain OVERWEIGHT on the Banking, Construction, EMS, Healthcare, Industrial (data centres), Oil and Gas, Renewable Energy, and Transport sectors, largely reflecting our conviction in industries that benefit from long-term policy and rising domestic investment. While we retain a neutral stance on the Technology sector, we see merit in selective exposure given its high beta and tendency to outperform during a market upcycle. Backing the structural winners Our strategy continues to favour a selective approach, focusing on durable growth themes that can withstand external headwinds, as we believe structural growth stories offer sustainable re-rating potential and tend to outperform over the long term. We prefer sectors supported by megatrends with strong earnings visibility and enduring tailwinds: 1) sustained FDI inflows, 2) acceleration in infrastructure investment, 3) supply chain reconfiguration, 4) energy transition, and 5) data centre expansion. Maintain Neutral with revised year-end KLCI target of 1,610 We reiterate our Neutral call on the market with a revised year-end KLCI target of 1,610 (from 1,700), based on a lower target PE multiple of 15x. Our KLCI EPS growth forecast has been reduced to 3.5%/5.6% for 2025/26E, compared to 7.4%/7.2% for our coverage universe. We downgrade the Gaming and Telecom sectors to NEUTRAL (from Overweight) and have recently initiated coverage on the Plantation sector. Notable changes in this report include our TP cuts across the Rubber Glove sector and downgrade on SunCon to Sell (from HOLD) following strong share price rally. Source:Phillip Capital Research- 23 Jun 2025

PUTRAJAYA (June 18): Malaysia’s highest court has ordered Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK) to cough up RM90 million to the National Feedlot Corporation (NFCorp) and four others, including NFCorp chairman Datuk Seri Mohamad Salleh Ismail. The plaintiff had sued Public Bank for leaking details of their accounts and a proposed purchase of properties at KL Eco City. The bank’s former clerk Johari Mohamad was implicated in the leak of confidential documents. The three-member bench at the Federal Court led by Chief Judge of Malaya Tan Sri Hasnah Mohammed Hashim awarded RM30 million each in equitable, exemplary and aggravated damages to the scandal-plagued firm after dismissing Public Bank’s appeal on May 26. Hasnah, who was flanked by Chief Judge of Sabah and Sarawak Tan Sri Abdul Rahman Sebli and judge Datuk Abu Bakar Jais, also ordered the bank to pay 2% interest on the total sum effective Wednesday, June 18, until the payment of damages is settled. Hasnah said that as for the general damages, the bench had calculated net income suffered by NFCorp as at the year 2013. She also added that the tabulation of quantification of exemplary and aggravated damages amounting to RM60 million were based on NFCorp’s audited accounts. “We have thoroughly examined the financial reports, projected income reports and audited accounts (of NFCorp) and award the following equitable damages of RM30 million, exemplary damages of RM30 million, and aggravated damages of RM30 million as well as 2% from the date of this order,” the judge said. Hasnah added that Public Bank had not rebutted or challenged the financial reports or audited accounts of NFCorp during its appeal. “We are of the view that the Court of Appeal erred when it found that NFCorp failed to prove damages and only awarded nominal damages of RM10,000 with interest. The Court of Appeal erred when it concluded that there were insufficient evidence adduced to support its claims of damages. On the contrary, NFCorp produced audited accounts and financial reports through its expert witnesses,” she said. She added that Public Bank did not rebut or challenge the reports and audited accounts through any witnesses or expert witnesses. “Public Bank only challenged the expertise and credibility of (NFCorp’s) expert witnesses,” she said. Brief history of NFCorp’s case from the High Court The originating suit in the High Court was filed by NFCorp, Mohamad Salleh, National Meat & Livestock Corporation Sdn Bhd, Agroscience Industries Sdn Bhd, and Real Food Company Sdn Bhd against Public Bank for leaking details of their accounts and the proposed purchase of properties at KL Eco City. The High Court did not find that Public Bank had breached confidentiality. On Aug 30, 2023, the Court of Appeal overturned the High Court’s decision, finding Public Bank liable for breaching its duty of confidentiality. The COA only awarded nominal damages of RM10,000 and ordered Public Bank to pay RM500,000 in costs. This resulted in NFCorp going to the Federal Court this year to appeal for more damages, while Public Bank appealed to reverse the COA’s decision against it, which led to Public Bank losing this appeal. In their decision on May 26, the bench only awarded NFCorp RM300,000 in costs by Public Bank, and set June 18 to deliver the bench’s decision on damages to be awarded. NFCorp’s lawyer Tan Sri Muhammad Shafee Abdullah told the bench previously that his clients were seeking RM560 million in damages: RM60 million in general damages, RM250 million in exemplary damages, and another RM250 million in aggravated damages. Shafee said that the COA and High Court judges did not consider that his client had suffered serious loss due to the bank’s breach of confidentiality. The COA, in overturning the High Court’s decision, ruled that the High Court judge had ignored vital evidence from the bank’s domestic inquiry on its former clerk Johari, who was implicated in the leak of confidential documents. Politician and former economy minister Datuk Seri Mohd Rafizi Ramli had at the time held a press conference to disclose the information along with NFCorp’s customer profile and balance summary, which led to Mohamad Salleh complaining on March 30, 2012 about wrongful disclosure and thus filing the suit. Source: TheEdge - 19 Jun 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (June 18): Probability of defaults among small and medium enterprises (SMEs) is likely to increase following the impact of US reciprocal tariffs, potentially pushing up credit costs for the banking sector, CIMB Securities has cautioned. Credit costs refer to provisions that banks set aside to cover potential bad loans. While direct trade-related loans remain minimal — accounting for just 1% to 4.7% of total loans across banks under coverage — CIMB said that spillover effects from weakening trade could strain SME suppliers and vendors along the supply chain. The SME segment, which makes up about 16.5% of total bank loans, could be the first to feel the pressure, the research house said in a note on Wednesday. Assuming the probability of default for SME loans increases from 4.5% to 6%, estimated credit losses could rise by 75 basis points (bps) for this segment. This, in turn, could result in a 12 bps increase in overall sector credit costs. Nevertheless, CIMB said the projection remains within its earlier assumption of a 15 bps increase in credit costs. As such, it is maintaining its forecast and ‘neutral’ sector rating. Despite the cautious view, the house retained 'buy' calls on Alliance Bank Malaysia Bhd (KL:ABMB), Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK), and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK), citing attractive dividend yields. CIMB also highlighted potential upside risks, including lower-than-expected credit losses, stronger net interest margins, and gains from non-interest income. Key downside risks, meanwhile, include higher funding costs, liquidity outflows, and a sharper deterioration in asset quality. Source: TheEdge - 19 Jun 2025

The FBM KLCI added 0.31 points to close at 1,511.95, after moving between an early low of 1,509.42 and high of 1,512.96, but losers edged gainers 488 to 361, on total turnover of 2.43bn shares worth RM1.8bn. Asian Markets Mixed on Geopolitical Concerns Stocks in Asia ended mixed on Wednesday as concerns about the escalating tensions in the Middle East weigh on traders’ sentiment. President Donald Trump met with his national security team to discuss the escalating Middle East conflict, according to people familiar with the matter, fueling fresh speculation that the US is on the verge of joining Israel’s attack on Iran. Traders also kept a close eye on economic data, with US retail sales down for a second month, suggesting anxiety over tariffs and their finances prompted consumers to pull back after an early-year spending rush. On economic front, Japanese manufacturers grew less confident about business conditions in June and expressed caution about the outlook for the next three months, citing U.S. tariff uncertainties and weak Chinese demand, a Reuters Tankan poll showed. Japan’s Nikkei 225 rose 0.90% to 38,885.15, while the Topix added 0.77% to 2,808.35. In Australia, the S&P/ASX 200 edged lower by 0.12% to 8,531.20, while South Korea’s Kospi gained 0.74% to 2,972.19. In mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index was virtually unchanged at 3,388.81, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index dropped 1.12% to 23,710.69. Wall Street Mixed as Fed Keeps Rates Unchanged Wall Street's major indexes closed with mixed results overnight as traders digested the Federal Reserve's decision to maintain interest rates at their current levels. The Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 0.10% 42,171.66. The S&P 500 slipped 0.03% to close at 5,980.87, and the Nasdaq Composite inched up 0.13% to settle at 19,546.27. The lacklustre close on Wall Street came after the Federal Reserve announced its widely expected decision to leave interest rates unchanged. The Fed left its key rate unchanged Wednesday in a range of 4.25% to 4.5%, as markets expected. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said monetary policy still needs to impose some restraint on the economy. Stocks have given up most of their gains this afternoon as Federal Reserve projections suggest slightly lower growth for the rest of the year and a bit more tariff-produced inflation. Nonetheless, most members of the Federal Open Market Committee see two rate cuts before the end of the year. Earlier in the day, initial jobless claims data showed the number of Americans filing new applications for unemployment benefits fell last week but stayed at levels consistent with a further loss of labour market momentum in June. (Bloomberg) Drop in Exports Raises Japan's Recession Risks as US Tariffs Bite Japan’s exports fell for the first time in eight months as the US tariff campaign weighed on global trade, raising the risk of a technical recession after the economy contracted at the start of the year. Exports measured by value dropped 1.7% in May from a year earlier led by cars, steel and mineral fuel, the Ministry of Finance reported on Wednesday. While the fall was smaller than the median analyst estimate of a 3.7% decline, data suggested exporters may be absorbing the tariff shock by cutting prices, as export volume rose 1.8%. Imports declined 7.7%, with crude oil and coal seeing some of the sharpest falls, leaving Japan’s trade balance at a deficit of JPY637.6bn (US$4.4bn). The shortfall widened from the previous month, and marked a second consecutive month of deficits. The drop in exports and the larger trade deficit add to concerns that Japan’s economy could shrink again in the second quarter, pushing it into a technical recession. Domestic consumption has remained weak as inflation continues to outpace wage hikes. (Bloomberg) Fed Officials Hold Rates Again, Still See Two Cuts by Year End Federal Reserve officials continued to pencil in two interest-rate cuts in 2025, though new projections showed a growing divide among policymakers over the trajectory for borrowing costs as tariffs make their way through the US economy. The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously on Wednesday to hold the benchmark federal funds rate in a range of 4.25%-4.5%, as they have since the beginning of the year. They also released new economic forecasts — their first since President Donald Trump unveiled a sweeping set of tariffs in April — showing they expect weaker growth, higher inflation and higher unemployment this year. Speaking to reporters following the decision, Chair Jerome Powell repeated his view that the central bank was “well positioned to wait to learn more about the likely course of the economy before considering any adjustments to our policy stance.” Interestrate projections released alongside the decision show a split: Seven officials now foresee no rate cuts this year, compared with four in March, and two others pointed to one cut. At the same time, 10 officials expect it will be appropriate to lower rates at least twice before the end of 2025. In the run-up to this month’s meeting, many officials signalled their preference to hold rates steady for some time as they wait for clarity on how Trump’s economic policies will affect inflation and the broader economy. In their updated economic forecasts, officials raised their median estimate for inflation at the end of 2025 to 3% from 2.7%. They marked down their forecast for economic growth in 2025 1.4% from 1.7%. They forecast an unemployment rate of 4.5% by the end of the year, up slightly from their previous estimate. Officials downgraded this year’s GDP growth forecast to 1.4%, vs. 1.7% in the March Summary of Economic Projections (SEP). (Bloomberg) Foreign Treasuries Holdings Held Near a Record in April Turmoil Foreign investors’ holdings of US Treasuries held close to a record high in April, despite the turmoil in financial markets in the wake of President Donald Trump’s plans for the biggest tariff hikes in more than a century. Foreign holdings totalled USD9.01tn for the month, the second-highest figure on record and down just USD36bn from March, Treasury Department figures showed Wednesday. The drop mainly reflected net sales by foreign private investors of US notes and bonds. Official entities were net buyers of longer-term Treasuries. Japan’s and Britain’s holdings increased, while China’s declined, the data showed. The drop in overall holdings met the expectation of some market participants. After Trump unveiled his “Liberation Day” tariffs April 2, stocks plunged — an event that typically sees haven demand for US government securities. Instead, Treasuries logged their deepest decline in more than two decades in the week after the trade-policy shock. With the dollar also tumbling, the moves sparked concerns about a wholesale exodus of foreign investors from American assets. The number of building permits issued in May fell to an annualized rate of 1.39mn, also a five-year low. (The Edge, Bernama) US Jobless Claims Stabilise Near Highest Levels This Year Applications for US unemployment benefits ticked down last week, stabilising near the highest levels in eight months. Initial claims decreased by 5,000 to 245,000 in the week ended June 14, in line with the median forecast in a Bloomberg survey of economists. Continuing claims, a proxy for the number of people receiving benefits, also fell slightly to 1.95mn, in the previous week, according to Labour Department data released Wednesday. Data tend to be volatile, especially around holidays or when schools are on summer vacation. The four-week moving average of new applications, a metric that helps smooth out volatility, rose to 245,500, the highest since August 2023. Before adjusting for seasonal factors, initial claims fell last week. Illinois, California and Georgia saw the largest declines. (Bloomberg) UK Inflation Slows in May But Food Prices Jump British inflation slowed as expected in May, pulled down by air fares which leapt in April and the correction of a tax data error, although food prices shot up at the fastest rate in more than a year. Consumer prices rose in annual terms by 3.4% in May, the Office for National Statistics (ONS) said on Wednesday, just as a Reuters poll of economists and the Bank of England (BOE) had predicted. Services price inflation — a crucial metric for the BOE — cooled to 4.7% from 5.4% in April, matching the BOE's forecast for May. The Reuters poll had pointed to a reading of 4.8%. Earlier this month the ONS said April's headline consumer price inflation reading of 3.5% had been overstated by 0.1 percentage points due to an error in car tax data from the government. April's figures were not amended, but the correct data was used for May's readings. Air fares fell sharply after an Easter holiday spike in April's readings. The data are unlikely to shift interest rate expectations among economists and investors who think the BOE will leave borrowing costs on hold when it announces its June policy decision on Thursday. (Reuters) UK Home Prices See Biggest Drop Since 2021 as Tax Hike Kicks in UK home prices fell in April by most since 2021, government data show, as a tax increase drove some buyers out of the market. The average price nationwide was £265,000 (US$356,150 or RM1.52mn), down 2.8% from March, according to figures published by the UK Office for National Statistics (ONS). It was the first decline since December 2023 and the largest since the pandemic. The ONS said the drop reversed the surge in March, when buyers rushed to tie up deals before the higher stamp-duty rates kicked in at the end of the month. House prices were up 3.5% in April from a year before, a marked slowdown from the pace in the previous few months. The latest figures add to reports that have showed a mixed view of the real estate market in recent months. While estate agents reported the weakest month for house sales in two years following the tax rise, figures from mortgagelender Halifax and property website Rightmove suggested prices had defied expectations of a slowdown in April. War Clouds Increased Downside Volatility The local blue-chip benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) engaged in choppy trade over the previous week, influenced by China’s sustained deflationary pressure and weaker than expected export growth, especially after shipments to the US plunged 34.5% YoY due to tariff. Investors were also sidelined while they kept a close eye on trade discussions between the US and China. Blue chips then made gains after both parties agreed to implement a trade framework that would ease export restrictions. However, the local benchmark index staged a profit-taking correction towards the end of the week amid rising tensions in the Middle East. Week-on-week, the FBM KLCI gained 1.32 points, or 0.09 percent to 1,518.11, as advances in YTL Corp (+12sen), YTL Power (+19sen), 99 Speed Mart (+7sen), QL Resources (+11sen) and Petronas Chemicals (+8sen) counteracted losses in Sime Darby (-11sen) and Nestle Malaysia (-RM3.30). Average daily traded volume last week increased to 2.84 billion shares versus the 2.46 billion shares the previous week, while average daily traded value grew to RM2.13 billion versus the RM2.05 billion average the previous week. Focus on Israel-Iran Conflict and Zafrul’s Visit to US The local market is expected to take its cue from the sharp correction in U.S. equity markets last Friday, triggered by escalating tensions between Israel and Iran. If the conflict spreads across the Middle East and disrupts global crude oil supply, it could have farreaching consequences for the broader economy. The situation could deteriorate further if Israel’s ally, the U.S., and Iran’s supporters, Russia and China, become directly involved. As a result, the benchmark FBMKLCI index may face downward pressure as investors pivot toward safe-haven assets. However, a glimmer of hope remains should both parties agree to a ceasefire and enter negotiations. Additionally, Investment, Trade, and Industry Minister Tengku Zafrul Aziz’s visit to Washington this week could bolster investor sentiment if trade discussions yield favourable outcomes. More Uncertainty and Volatility Israel launched preemptive military strikes against Iran on June 12, 2025, citing existential threats following repeated warnings from Iran’s political and military leadership to annihilate Israel. The operation, dubbed "Rising Lion," targeted Iran’s nuclear infrastructure, military installations, key leadership figures, and nuclear scientists. The US has denied any involvement and said Israel acted unilaterally for self-defence. However, Iran's army is not convinced that the US is not involved. This conflict adds further uncertainty to an already strained geopolitical landscape, with Russia’s war in Ukraine escalating, India and Pakistan experiencing renewed tensions following their brief four-day war, and China’s territorial disputes with multiple Asian nations intensifying. The war is expected to drive up oil prices, disrupt global supply chains, exert inflationary pressure on goods and services, and potentially hinder any Federal Reserve easing in 2025. On the first day of the attack, financial markets remained relatively muted, but volatility surged on the second day, as evidenced by contractions across all tracked markets. Meanwhile, commodity prices particularly gold and crude oil spiked in response to heightened geopolitical uncertainty. Comparing this to the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, the initial reaction was mostly muted but prices of crude oil and gold jumped. However, within a month, most financial markets recovered and moved higher in the next three months, with notable exceptions like Hong Kong and China, which continued to struggle. The current conflict, however, presents a fundamentally different risk profile. Unlike the Israel-Hamas war, which involved a state and a militant group, the Israel-Iran conflict now engages two nations with strong military capabilities, backed by global superpowers. This heightened geopolitical complexity could lead to prolonged market volatility, particularly in energy markets, as crude oil prices have already surged. Tehran’s regional influence has eroded following Israel’s successful efforts to significantly weaken Iran-backed groups, including Hamas in Gaza and Hezbollah in Lebanon, while also intensifying attacks on the Houthis in Yemen. Additionally, the U.S.-brokered strategic agreement with Syria’s new leadership, Ahmed al-Sharaa, after the fall of Bashar al-Assad in December 2024, has further disrupted Iran’s regional supply chains, particularly its ability to fund and arm Hezbollah and other allied factions. With diminished geopolitical leverage, Tehran has been forced to reassess its regional strategy, and reports indicate that it may have accelerated its nuclear program as an alternative deterrent. Israeli intelligence assessments—though unverified—allege that Iran possesses enough enriched uranium to produce multiple nuclear warheads within weeks, a justification Israel used for its June 12 military action. With limited regional support, the destruction of its military and nuclear facilities, and the elimination of key military leaders and nuclear scientists, Iran may turn to global allies such as China, Russia, and North Korea for assistance. However, Russia’s ability to provide undivided support is constrained by its ongoing war in Ukraine, where it is also receiving aid from China and North Korea. This could limit their involvement in the Israel-Iran conflict. Nevertheless, this does not necessarily indicate a swift resolution, as Iran retaliated on a massive scale on Saturday, prompting Israel to vow further countermeasures. Meanwhile, U.S. President Trump and Russian President Putin have spoken over the phone about ending the conflict through negotiations, but Iran remains unwilling to return to the discussion table on its nuclear program following Israel’s preemptive strike. As a result, the war could last for several months and may escalate further if additional nations become involved. Crude Oil Supply Disruptions Can Boost Inflation and Affect Global Economy Iran, a member of the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), produces approximately 3.3 million barrels per day (bpd) and exports over 2 million bpd of crude oil and refined fuels. Among OPEC members, Saudi Arabia and the UAE are the only producers with spare capacity that can be activated immediately—2.5 million bpd and 1 million bpd, respectively. Outside OPEC, Russia faces limitations due to sanctions, while the U.S. could contribute an additional 1.2 million bpd. While this combined capacity could theoretically offset disruptions, Iran has previously warned that it would retaliate against any suppliers filling gaps left by sanctions or attacks. Given these uncertainties, oil prices could surge beyond USD90 per barrel, potentially surpassing USD 100 per barrel if Iran’s oil infrastructure is severely impacted. The inflationary impact would escalate dramatically if Iran blocks the Strait of Hormuz, the world’s most critical oil shipping lane. While Tehran has repeatedly threatened to do so, physically sealing off the strait is nearly impossible. However, targeting cargo ships passing through could provoke severe retaliation, particularly from the U.S., which may deploy its aircraft carriers to de-escalate tensions. The Strait of Hormuz, located between Oman and Iran, connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea. Approximately 20.8 million barrels of crude, condensate, and refined fuels pass through daily, making it irreplaceable in the short term. In response to potential disruptions, countries will scramble to find alternative routes, ramp up production, and release strategic reserves. This situation echoes the 1973 oil crisis, when the Organisation of Arab Petroleum Exporting Countries imposed an embargo on the U.S. in retaliation for its military support of Israel. The embargo disrupted 5 million barrels of daily supply, causing oil prices to quadruple. A similar scenario today could push crude oil prices toward USD150 per barrel, albeit temporarily, before inflationary pressures and demand destruction trigger a correction. We are maintaining our current crude oil price assumption of USD73/bbl in 2025 pending clarity on current turmoil. Israel-Iran War and Tariff Impact on Our FBMKLCI Target Ourbase-case FBMKLCI target of 1,660for 2025 assumes a favourable trade resolution that will retain the current 10% tariff while allowing greater flexibility for U.S. exporters to penetrate the Malaysian market and geopolitical tensions will be contained. Malaysia is expected to secure a more advantageous trade arrangement relative to most ASEAN peers—except Singapore and the Philippines—given its comparatively lower net exports to the U.S., which reduces exposure to tariff-related downside risks. Conversely, ourworst-case scenario of 1,580, based on minus 1-std deviation (CY26 EPS of 115.93 and PER of 13.6x) to long-term mean PER of 16x, assumes a broad-based 24% tariff reinstatement, with exemptions granted only to semiconductor products and select components, and the latest conflict between Israel and Iran is contained. Higher tariff likely to weigh on market sentiment, triggering risk-off positioning and a recalibration of earnings expectations across key sectors. The shift in risk appetite could be drastic if the Israel-Iran war escalates and prolongs, potentially engulfing other Middle Eastern nations.If alliances solidify—with the U.S. backingIsrael and Russia supporting Iran—the June 2023 low of 1,369.41 could be retested, with 1,300 acting as a critical support level,based on minus two standard deviations from the long-term mean.However, under such extreme geopolitical uncertainty, predicting the absolute market bottom remains highly speculative and the 2020 low of 1,207 during Covid could act as a strong support. Its floating production storage and offloading units are crucial for brownfield redevelopment projects as well.VELESTO’s rigs are engaged in drilling and maintenance in brownfield sites while its Workover & Intervention Services provides hydraulic workover units, enabling recompletion and intervention to extend the life of aging wells.
