SCIB affirms that the discontinuation of the separate action does not reflect a withdrawal or abandonment of the company's claim.(New Straits Times) Ramssol Group Bhd (Not Rated)is eyeing potential revenue of 1bn Thai baht, or about RM131.6mn, over the next three years from AI solutions in Thailand. Geekstart Co Ltd, a Thailand-based software company in which Ramssol has a 51% stake, signed a partnership agreement with King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology Ladkrabang (KMITL) to roll out AI solutions to both the private and public sectors in Thailand. The alliance will operate under the RAMS AI Tech brand.(The Edge) MN Holdings Bhd's (Not Rated)unit, MN Power Transmission Sdn Bhd (MNPTSB), has secured two contracts worth RM29.7mn from Fabulous Sunview Sdn Bhd (FSSB), a wholly owned subsidiary of Sunview Group Bhd. MN Holdings said MNPTSB has been appointed as the interconnection facilities contractor for two 29.99 Megawatt alternating current (MWac) solar power plant projects in Sungai Petani, Kedah. The scope includes the establishment of a new IF and the extension of two 132 kilovolt bays at the existing main intake substation Aman Jaya.(New Straits Times) GDEX Bhd (Not Rated)is starting to recover after diversifying into the IT services sector, which offers better margins. The company had faced significant challenges over the past three years, including intense competition from foreign logistics firms. To adapt, GDEX transitioned from a pure express delivery provider to a broader service portfolio that now includes logistics and IT services.(The Edge) Berjaya Food Bhd (Not Rated)(BFood) will open two Starbucks stores in Reykjavik, Iceland, next month under a licensing agreement with Starbucks Coffee Company. This marks the group's first entry into the Icelandic coffee market via its subsidiary, Berjaya Food International (BFI). The first outlet will open in May on Reykjavik's main shopping street, followed by a second in the city's new harbour district. Exact dates and addresses were not disclosed.(New Straits Times) IGB REIT (Not Rated)andIGB Commercial REIT (Not Rated)reported higher earnings in 1QFY25 but warned investors of challenges ahead. The two REITs, which run malls and office buildings, said economic uncertainty and possible fuel subsidy cuts may lower mall spending and hurt tenants, while office space faces too much supply and demand for newer buildings.(The Edge) Axis Real Estate Investment Trust's (Not Rated)net income rose 16.2% to RM49.1mn in 1QFY25 from RM42.3mn in 1QFY24, as new acquisitions, tenancies and rental rates boosted its property income. Quarterly earnings per unit rose to 2.44 sen from 2.42 sen in 1QFY24. The commercial and industrial REIT declared a dividend of 2.5 sen per unit, up from 2.3 sen last year.(The Edge) United Plantations Bhd (Not Rated)posted a higher net profit of RM163.3mn for 1QFY25, up from RM133.5mn a year earlier. Revenue for the quarter rose by 8.6% to RM517.6mn from RM476.8mn previously. The company said that revenue growth was mainly driven by increases in the plantation and refinery segments during the quarter, primarily due to higher crude palm oil and palm kernel production and prices. The company's profit before tax at RM222.4mn for the current quarter was also higher by 24.6% compared to RM178.4mn in the corresponding quarter, mainly due to a higher contribution from the plantation segment.(New Straits Times) Economy IMF Cuts Real GDP Growth Forecast for Malaysia to 4.1% in 2025 The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has downgraded its real gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast for Malaysia to 4.1% this year, from its January estimate of 4.7%, in line with a broader reduction in regional projections. In its April 2025 World Economic Outlook, titled "A Critical Juncture amid Policy Shifts", the IMF also forecast Malaysia’s real GDP to expand by 3.8% in 2026. The IMF trimmed its global growth projection for 2025 to 2.8%, down 0.5 percentage point from the January forecast. Among Malaysia’s regional peers, the IMF cut Indonesia’s 2025 growth outlook to 4.7% from 5.1%. The Philippines is now expected to grow by 5.5%, compared with 6.1% earlier, while Thailand’s growth forecast was revised down to 1.8% from 2.9%. The fund said major policy shifts are reshaping the global trade landscape and reigniting uncertainty, once again testing the global economy’s resilience. “Since February, the US has announced multiple waves of tariffs against trading partners, some of which have invoked countermeasures. Markets initially took the announcements mostly in stride, until the US’ near-universal application of tariffs on April 2, which triggered historic drops in major equity indices and spikes in bond yields, followed by a partial recovery after the pause and additional carve-outs announced on and after April 9,” it said. (The Edge, IMF) Malaysia's consumer inflation slowest in four years as non-food price gain eases Malaysia’s headline inflation decelerated to its slowest in four years as price gains at restaurants and of other non-food items eased. The consumer price index (CPI) rose 1.4% in March when compared to the same month in 2024, the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) said in a statement on Wednesday. The print was lowest since February 2021, and undershot the median 1.6% rise projected in a Bloomberg survey. On a month-onmonth basis, the inflation gauge was down 0.1% in March. The restaurant and accommodation services group eased to 2.9%, down from a 3.5% rise in February. Prices of personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services rose 3.6%, a slight slowdown versus the 3.7% increase in February. The price increase for housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels came in slower at 1.9%, compared with the 2.3% jump a month ago. Prices of alcoholic beverages and tobacco increased 0.8%, easing from a 0.9% rise in February. (The Edge, DOSM) Malaysia-Türkiye to Strengthen Energy Sector Cooperation — Fadillah Malaysia and Türkiye are working to move beyond an exchange of letter of intent related to energy sector cooperation, particularly in renewable and nuclear energy for power supply purposes, into a memorandum of understanding (MOU), said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof. Fadillah, who is also the energy transition and water transformation minister, said this would pave the way for cooperation between high-level officials and policymakers from both countries. He said this was among the matters discussed during his meeting with Türkiye’s Energy and Natural Resources Minister Alparslan Bayraktar in Ankara on Tuesday (April 22). "We are working to upgrade this letter of intent into an MOU," he told Bernama. The exchange of the letter of intent was formalised during Türkiye’s President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s Feb 10-11 official visit to Malaysia. (The Edge, Bernama) Indonesia Seeks Room for Rate Cuts as Tariffs Dim Growth Outlook Indonesia’s central bank will keep looking for an opportunity to cut interest rates when the currency allows it, as policymakers flagged early signs of the global trade war hitting the nation’s economic growth and bank lending. In the short-term, though, Bank Indonesia will prioritise rupiah stability and keep intervening to support Asia’s worst-performing currency as US President Donald Trump’s tariff threats likely stir more volatility. The rupiah’s slump stayed BI’s hand for a third straight month on Wednesday as it kept the benchmark BI-Rate unchanged at 5.75%. The move was predicted by 27 of 29 economists surveyed by Bloomberg, while two expected a quarter-point cut. “We will continue to monitor the scope for interest rate cuts,” governor Perry Warjiyo said in an online briefing from Washington DC, where an Indonesian delegation is also in town for tariff talks. “Once rupiah stability is achieved, room for further cuts will become more open, and that will be the time.” BI will closely monitor inflation and growth prospects in considering rate cuts, while taking into account movements in the rupiah, the governor said from Washington, where he is attending the World Bank and International Monetary Fund meetings. (Bloomberg) India Says Tariffs Could Shave Half Percentage Point Off Growth US tariffs on India could shave up to half a percentage point off the country’s economic growth, a top Indian government official said, adding that a more significant problem would be the global economy slumping. The “first order” hit from US reciprocal tariffs on India, if imposed, could reduce the nation’s gross domestic product by between 0.2 and 0.5 percentage points, according to Economic Affairs Secretary Ajay Seth. He categorized that figure as having “not a huge impact.” “But the second order would be important,” Seth said in Washington on Wednesday. “The second order is if the global economy grows less” than expected. The Indian government is maintaining its growth outlook for the 2025-2026 fiscal year of 6.3% to 6.8%, Seth said. The IMF lowered its 2025 forecast for the South Asian nation to 6.2%. Seth said it’s too soon to revise the $4tn economy’s outlook because its still negotiating to avoid 26% levies on exports to the US, one of its biggest trading partners. He’s optimistic about a deal with Washington, though he gave no specific details on the talks. (Bloomberg) Singapore Inflation Hits Four-year Low as Living Costs Ease Singapore’s key inflation gauge fell to its lowest since March 2021 as the prices of utilities, as well as household goods and services slowed. The core inflation rate, which excludes housing and private transportation costs, stood at 0.5% in March from a year earlier, lower than the 0.6% in February, according to a statement by the Department of Statistics on Wednesday. That compares against the median estimate of 0.7% in a Bloomberg News survey and extends a trend of deceleration that began in October. Overall inflation came in at 0.9% last month, same as February’s pace and lower than the 1.1% median estimate. Cost-of-living pressures have been a key focus of the government, with its budget for this year providing cash handouts and rebates for Singaporeans still feeling the price pain. The city-state is set to hold elections on May 3. The Monetary Authority of Singapore further revised down its forecast for this year’s core inflation outlook during its April policy review to average 0.5%-1.5% this year from a previous estimate of 1%-2%. The headline inflation estimate was also cut to 0.5%- 1.5% from 1.5%-2.5% earlier. (Bloomberg) Thailand May Need US$15bn of Stimulus to Cope With US Tariffs Thailand may need at least THB500bn (US$15bn) for economic stimulus measures to address the impact of US tariffs, its finance minister said on Wednesday. The measures will focus on boosting domestic consumption and investment, as well as providing soft loans, Pichai Chunhavajira told reporters. Thailand is among Southeast Asian nations hardest hit by US President Donald Trump's measures, facing a 36% tariff if a reduction can't be negotiated before a global moratorium expires in July. he country's negotiations with Washington on the tariff were postponed because the United States has asked Bangkok to review important issues. The government is closely monitoring the situation and is prepared to implement measures to stimulate the economy and maintain growth levels, Pichai said. Discussions with relevant agencies, including the National Economic and Social Development Council and the Bank of Thailand, are ongoing on the measures, which will be funded from several sources, he added. "The economic stimulus package will have to be large enough to create momentum," Pichai said. When the 36% tariff was announced this month, Pichai had said it could cut growth in Southeast Asia's second-largest economy by 1 percentage point. The economy grew just 2.5% last year, lagging regional peers. (Reuters) Trade Worries Dominate Fed’s Beige Book, Activity Little Changed Uncertainty over President Donald Trump’s heightened use of tariffs on US trading partners loomed large across the country in recent weeks, according to the Federal Reserve’s Beige Book survey of regional contacts. “Economic activity was little changed since the previous report, but uncertainty around international trade policy was pervasive across reports,” the survey released Wednesday said. Tariff mentions came up 107 times in the Fed report, more than double the number seen in the prior Beige Book. Variations of the word “uncertain” appeared 89 times. The outlook “worsened considerably” in several regions as economic uncertainty — especially around tariffs — increased, according to the report. The Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta compiled the latest edition of the Beige Book using information gathered on or before April 14. The report includes commentary and anecdotes about business conditions in each of the Fed’s 12 regional districts, collected directly from businesses and other contacts. Fed officials next meet May 6-7. (Bloomberg) US Mortgage Rates Rise Again, Reach Highest Since Mid-February US mortgage rates rose again last week, reaching the highest level since mid-February and further depressing the appetite to buy homes or refinance loans. The contract rate on a 30- year mortgage climbed nine basis points to 6.90% in the week ended April 18, according to Mortgage Bankers Association data released Wednesday. That followed a 20 basis-point jump in the prior week, marking the biggest back-to-back increase since early November. Mortgage rates track US Treasury yields, which have risen recently as they lose their appeal as a safe haven. MBA’s index applications for home purchases fell for a second week to the lowest level also since February. The refinancing gauge plunged 20%, the most this year. Data on March new-home sales will be released later Wednesday, while figures on the previously owned homes market are due Thursday. The MBA survey, which has been conducted weekly since 1990, uses responses from mortgage bankers, commercial banks and thrifts. The data cover more than 75% of all retail residential mortgage applications in the US. (Bloomberg) Trump Now Says He Has No Intention of Firing Fed Chair Powell US President Donald Trump said he had no intention of firing Federal Reserve (Fed) chair Jerome Powell despite his frustration with the central bank not moving more quickly to slash interest rates. “Never did,” Trump told reporters on Tuesday. “The press runs away with things. No, I have no intention of firing him. I would like to see him be a little more active in terms of his idea to lower interest rates.” Trump’s National Economic Council director Kevin Hassett last Friday told reporters that Trump was studying the question of whether he is able to fire Powell after a series of presidential social media posts and public comments criticising the Fed. The president unleashed a tirade against Powell last week right before the European Central Bank lowered its benchmark rate by a quarter point to 2.25%, about half the Fed’s rate of 4.25-4.5%, saying in a post on Truth Social that “Powell’s termination cannot come fast enough”. The US president has repeatedly complained that the Fed was not cutting interest rates quickly enough. Trump reiterated that criticism on Tuesday, even as he insisted the controversy over his remarks — which rattled markets — was overblown. (Bloomberg) New Home Sales Surge Much More Than Expected in March New home sales in the U.S. surged by much more than expected in the month of March, according to a report released by the Commerce Department on Wednesday. The Commerce Department said new home sales spiked by 7.4% to an annual rate of 724,000 in March after jumping by 3.1% to a revised rate of 674,000 in February. Economists had expected new home sales to climb by 0.9% to a rate of 682,000 from the 676,000 originally reported for the previous month. With the much bigger than expected increase, new home sales reached their highest level since hitting an annual rate of 726,000 last September. New home sales in the South led the way higher, soaring by 13.6% to an annual rate of 483,000. The report said new home sales in the Midwest also shot up by 3.0% to an annual rate of 69,000. Meanwhile, new home sales in the West slumped by 1.4% to an annual rate of 144,000, and new home sales in the Northeast plunged by 22.2% to an annual rate of 28,000. (RTT) Growth in US Business Activity Weakest Since 2023 on Tariffs US business activity expanded at the slowest pace since 2023, while output expectations deteriorated and price pressures mounted as tariffs created headaches for companies. The S&P Global flash April composite index of output at manufacturers and services providers dropped 2.3 points to 51.2, according to data released Wednesday. Figures above 50 indicate expansion. A gauge of future production slid 3.5 points to the lowest level since October 2022. “Manufacturing is broadly stagnating as any beneficial effect of tariffs are offset by heightened economic uncertainty, supply chain concerns and falling exports, while the services economy is slowing amid weakened demand growth, notably in terms of exports such as travel and tourism,” Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said in a statement. Complicating matters is an acceleration in prices charged, with the composite gauge climbing to a more than one-year high. The pickup was attributed to tariffs, higher import prices and rising labour costs. (Bloomberg) Eurozone Business Activity Stagnates as Trade Woes Hit Services Private-sector activity in the euro area barely grew in April as tariff uncertainty sent confidence in the services industry to an almost five-year low. The Composite Purchasing Managers’ Index by S&P Global fell to 50.1 from 50.9 in March, remaining narrowly above the 50-threshold separating expansion from contraction, data Wednesday showed. Analysts had predicted a drop to 50.2. The deterioration was largely down to Germany, whose own main PMI gauge unexpectedly declined to less than 50 for the first time in four months. France also missed analyst estimates, remaining stuck beneath that level. Both of Europe’s two biggest economies saw surprise weakness in services. (Bloomberg) ECB Predicts Wage Growth Will Slow Sharply at End of This Year The European Central Bank’s main gauge of future pay growth continued to indicate a sharp slowdown in 2025, underpinning expectations for a further retreat in inflation that may allow more interest-rate cuts. The ECB’s wage tracker, published Wednesday, predicts salaries will rise by an annual 1.6% in the fourth quarter. That’s just above the 1.5% projection seen in March, and a far cry from the 5.3% peak recorded last year. The ECB this month lowered borrowing costs for a seventh time but won’t commit to further steps amid what officials have described as “exceptional” uncertainty around US trade policy. Inflation eased to 2.2% in March, with president Christine Lagarde saying the task of hitting the 2% target is “nearing completion.” Prices in the services sector, where salaries play a large role, are still rising rapidly. But those gains have also leveled off in recent months. Lagarde has said “wages are gradually moderating.” Backing this, an ECB survey published Tuesday showed greater confidence among companies that pay growth will retreat further — reaching 3% and 2.5% in 2025 and 2026 – down from 4.3% in 2024. The figure for 2025 was 0.5 percentage point lower than in earlier survey rounds, the ECB said. (Bloomberg) UK company activity weakens by most in more than two years as US trade war rages, PMI shows British companies buckled in April under the strain of an escalating global trade war that threatens to tip the economy into a new downturn, a survey showed on Wednesday. The S&P Global Composite Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), a gauge of the private sector economy, slid to 48.2 in April from 51.5 in March. This marked the lowest reading since November 2022 when businesses were wracked by surging energy costs and financial market turmoil after former Prime Minister Liz Truss' poorly received budget plans. Readings below 50 denote a contraction in business activity. Export orders fell at the fastest pace since the early months of the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020, while costs faced by businesses grew at the fastest rate in more than two years as higher employment taxes and an increased minimum wage kicked in. (Reuters) UK Budget Deficit Overshot Forecast Even Before Tariffs Hit UK government borrowing exceeded official forecasts made just last month, highlighting the fragile state of the public finances even before US tariffs dimmed the economic outlook. The budget deficit in the fiscal year through March was GBP151.9bn, the Office for National Statistics said Wednesday. That’s more than the GBP137.3bn forecast by the Office for Budget Responsibility, a figure that itself had been upwardly revised. Borrowing in March alone was GBP16.4bn. Economists surveyed by Bloomberg expected a deficit of GBP15.8bn on average. Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves cut spending and raised taxes last month to prevent a breach of her fiscal rules. However, there are fears that she may have to announce further pain later this year as the trade war launched by Donald Trump threatens to hit the world economy. On Tuesday, the International Monetary Fund downgraded UK growth prospects over the next two years by more than any leading European nation, raising fresh doubts over whether the deficit will fall as much in the current fiscal year as forecast. Reeves is in Washington this week where she will appeal to her US counterpart Scott Bessent for a deal to limit the direct impact of Trump’s tariffs. (Bloomberg) IMF Says Tariff Pressures to Push Global Public Debt Past Pandemic Levels Economic pressures from steep new US tariffs will push global public debt above pandemicera levels to nearly 100% of global GDP by the end of the decade as slower growth and trade strain government budgets, the International Monetary Fund said on Wednesday. The IMF's latest Fiscal Monitor projected that global public debt would grow 2.8 percentage points to 95.1% of global GDP in 2025. It said the upward trend was likely to continue, reaching 99.6% of global GDP by 2030. Global public debt peaked in 2020 at 98.9% of GDP as governments borrowed heavily for Covid-19 relief and output shrank. Debt fell 10 percentage points within two years. But it has been edging back up and the latest forecast showed it accelerating. "Major tariff announcements by the United States, countermeasures by other countries, and exceptionally high levels of policy uncertainty, are contributing to worsening prospects and heightened risks," the IMF said in the report. It added that this leaves governments with more difficult trade-offs as their budgets are stretched by higher defense spending needs, demands for more social support and rising debt service costs that could grow with more inflationary pressures. Governments' annual fiscal deficits are forecast to average 5.1% of GDP in 2025, compared to 5.0% in 2024, 3.7% in 2022 and 9.5% in 2020, according to the report. (Reuters) Source:TA Research- 24 Apr 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Malaysia’s richest just got richer thanks to the stronger ringgit. The collective wealth of Malaysia’s top 50 tycoons rose to US$90 billion (RM396.9 billion) in 2025 from US$83.4 billion in the previous year thanks to a 5% appreciation in the local currency in the past 12 months, according to a statement from theForbesmagazine. Robert Kuok, aged 101, was still Malaysia's richest individual with a net worth of US$11.4 billion, leading an agribusiness-to-hotels empire under his Kuok Group. Tan Sri Quek Leng Chan, the patriarch of the Hong Leong Group, remained second with his fortune rising by US$1 billion to US$9.8 billion, driven by strong performance across the conglomerate that spans from banks to cement and from real estate to food. The Teh siblings, heirs to the late Tan Sri Teh Hong Piow ofPublic Bank Bhd(KL:PBBANK), held onto third place with their collective wealth growing 9% to US$5.9 billion. The list was compiled based on shareholding and financial data from various sources including stock exchanges, company reports, analysts, and government records. Net worths were calculated using stock prices and exchange rates as of market close on April 7, 2025. The ranking lists both individual and family fortunes, including those shared among relatives and may differ from the World’s Billionaires List, which includes individual fortunes with net worths as of March 7. Overall, 30 of the 50 wealthiest Malaysians are better off from a year ago. Among them are Tan Sri Paul Koon Poh Keong and his siblings runningPress Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd(KL:PMETAL), who moved up one spot to the fourth place after racking up a net worth of US$5.4 billion. New additions to the list of billionaires include Lee Thiam Wah, the founder of99 Speed Mart Retail Holdings Bhd(KL:99SMART), at number eight with US$3.5 billion after the listing of the country’s largest minimart chain. Source: TheEdge - 18 Apr 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (April 16): Malaysian banks, in spite of their domestic focus, will not emerge completely unscathed from the escalating international trade tensions, TA Securities flagged on Wednesday. Loans growth and fee-based income could potentially soften from delays in capital investments, expansion plans, and merger-and-acquisition activities as business sentiment weakens and visibility diminishes, the research house said in a sector note. “We believe that in the event of a potential economic slowdown, business loans are likely to be affected first,” TA Securities said. “Meanwhile, retail loans should remain steady as this segment of loans is diversified across individual borrowers and is often tied to essential needs like a mortgage.” TA Securities joins CIMB Securities and other research houses warning of potential impact to Malaysia’s banking sector amid US trade policy flip-flops. The US is currently investigating imports of semiconductors and pharmaceuticals on grounds that extensive reliance on foreign production of medicine and chips is a national security threat. The investigation follows exemptions announced over the weekend for consumer electronics and semiconductor equipment from the punitive reciprocal tariffs levied on goods shipped from all of US trading partners. A broader regional slowdown could also raise asset quality risks, particularly for banks with clients in export-reliant sectors, TA Securities said. Electrical and electronics, palm oil, machinery and equipment, rubber, furniture, and plastics — which collectively account for over 40% of Malaysia’s exports — are susceptible to softer external demand and rising input costs, potentially straining their cash flows, it noted. Banking stocks have declined amid the market volatility given their close link to overall health of the economy. Year-to-date,CIMB Group Holdings Bhd(KL:CIMB) was the biggest decliner among the 11 banks with an 18% loss.RHBBank Bhd(KL:RHBBANK) was the sole stock in the sector in the positive territory, eking out a 2.5% gain. Still, TA Securities is keeping Malaysian banks on ‘overweight’ as recent corrections brought valuations to more “compelling levels” that offer attractive entry points for many stocks in the sector. For strategy, the research house has ‘buy’ calls across all the banks under its coverage, with CIMB andPublic BankBhd(KL:PBBANK) as top sector picks. Source: TheEdge - 17 Apr 2025

Trade War Tensions Macro uncertainties have intensified amid shifting global trade dynamics, especially following erratic policy signals from the U.S. The FBM KLCI fell to a 21-month low in tandem with regional selloffs, as escalating trade tensions cast a shadow over Malaysia’s export-reliant economy. TA estimates that a 24% tariff on Malaysia’s exports to the U.S. could shave 1.2%-points off GDP on a full-year basis. Indirect Pressure on Banking Earnings Banking stocks have come under pressure amid heightened market volatility, as the sector’s outlook remains tightly linked to overall economic performance. While Malaysian banks are predominantly domestically focused and thus insulated from direct fallout from the U.S.-China trade tensions, we expect several indirect headwinds to emerge. Chief among them is the potential softening of loan and fee-based income growth as business sentiment weakens and visibility diminishes—leading to delays in capital investments, expansion plans, and M&A activities. Our loan growth forecast of 6.2% for 2025 could soften to between 5.5% to 5.8% on the back of weaker demand. Furthermore, a broader regional slowdown could elevate asset quality risks, particularly for banks with clients operating in export-reliant sectors such as electrical and electronics, palm oil, machinery and equipment, rubber, furniture, and plastics. These industries collectively account for over 40% of Malaysia’s exports and are vulnerable to softer external demand and margin compression from rising input costs, which could erode profitability and strain borrower cash flows. While we have conservatively raised our credit charge forecast for the sector to 30.6 bps in CY25 (up from 29.4 bps in CY24), there is potential for further increases. However, we note that any uptick may be contained, given the substantial overlay buffers accumulated by the banks over the years. Banks Best Positioned to Weather Tariff Uncertainties That said, we believe banks with the highest domestic and retail exposure, as well as the strongest asset quality, particularly those with ample loan loss coverage, are best positioned to weather current uncertainties. We believe that in the event of a potential economic slowdown, business loans are likely to be affected first. Meanwhile, retail loans should remain steady as this segment of loans is diversified across individual borrowers and is often tied to essential needs like a mortgage or HP. Moreover, business loans tend to be more susceptible to defaults in a volatile market environment, prompting banks to adopt a more cautious approach toward this segment. Based on the chart below, Public Bank stands out in this regard. With a predominantly local loan book with a substantial retail exposure (65% of loan book) and one of the sector's highest loan loss coverage ratios, we expect it to be least affected by trade-related risks. While Hong Leong Bank (HLBB) also scores well in the upper right-hand quadrant of the chart, we remain slightly cautious due to its earnings exposure to Bank of Chengdu (BoCD), which contributes around 28% of HLBB's PBT. Slower economic growth in China could pose a downside risk to this contribution. Nevertheless, we are keeping our earnings forecasts unchanged for now, as we believe the 90- day pause and temporary exemptions on selected export items could soften the overall impact of tariffs on 2025 performance. In the short term, we also expect a pick-up in front-loading activities during this window, which could lift second-quarter GDP numbers and partially offset potential weakness in the second half. Moreover, Malaysia may attract more trade orders and foreign investment thanks to its relatively lower tariff profile compared to some regional peers. Valuations Trimmed, But Opportunities Emerging We recently revised our 2025 FBMKLCI target downward to 1,660 (from 1,785). This is based on a CY26 price-to-earnings (PER) multiple of 14.1x. In tandem, we are adjusting our valuation assumption for the banking sector by raising the market risk premium from 5.5% to 6.0%, reflecting increased earnings risk, softer investor sentiment, and a more volatile macro backdrop. We maintain ourOVERWEIGHTstance on the banking sector. The recent correction has brought valuations to more compelling levels, with most banks now trading near their 10-year mean or -1 standard deviation on a price-to-book (P/B) basis, offering attractive entry points for many banking stocks. Overall, our top picks for the sector are: 1)CIMBdue to the stock’s attractive valuations. CIMB is currently trading near its 10-year mean P/B of ~1.0x, in line with peers and at a discount to larger competitors (average ~1.2x). We like CIMB for its Forward30 strategy, which lays out pragmatic growth targets through 2030, supported by improving asset quality and solid capital buffers. We believe the group is well-placed to tap into intra-ASEAN trade flows through its regional footprint, and 2)Public Bank, a defensive name backed by exceptional asset quality, and operational efficiency, with loan growth led by retail and SME lending. Its 0.4% impaired loan ratio, 153.6% loan loss coverage, and the best-in-class cost-to-income ratio of 34.5% make it a compelling pick for financial stability and consistent dividends. We also maintain BUY calls across all banks under our coverage. We take this opportunity to upgrade AMMB to BUY (from HOLD), as its risk-reward profile has improved significantly, offering over 12% potential upside. Nevertheless, key downside risks to sector earnings and share price performance include: 1) continued foreign fund outflows, 2) compressed net interest margins (NIMs) should Bank Negara Malaysia cut the OPR to support growth, and 3) higherthan-expected credit costs in a weakening economic environment. Source:TA Research- 16 Apr 2025

FBMKLCI Target Lowered to 1,660 due to Uncertainty and Policy Fluctuations Heightened uncertainty surrounding the impact of US President Donald Trump’s reciprocal tariffs coming into effect weighed down the local blue-chip benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) last week, as the index fell to a 21-month low, in line with steep selloffs in the region. While blue chips experienced a short-lived relief rally after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on additional tariffs for most countries, they quickly pulled back on mounting worries over the escalating US-China trade tensions. Week-on-week, the FBM KLCI lost 49.38 points, or 3.28 percent to 1,454.76, as declines in Press Metal Aluminium Holdings (-49sen), YTL Corp (-16sen), Gamuda (-28sen), YTL Power (-20sen) and Petronas Chemicals (-19sen) overshadowed gains on Nestle Malaysia (+RM6.12), Axiata (+8en) and Petronas Dagangan (+76sen). Average daily traded volume last week rose to 3.93 billion shares versus 2.23 billion shares the previous week, while average daily traded value climbed to RM3.51 billion from the RM1.91 billion average the previous week. Trading range for the blue-chip benchmark index last week expanded to 88.68 points, against the 25.62-point range the previous week, as it hit a low of 1,386.63 spurred by profit-taking activity in the energy, banking, and industrial products sectors. President Donald Trump threw a wet blanket on a possible relief rally in Bursa Malaysia, particularly tech stocks, this week after his U-turn on a tariff exempt announcement late last Friday evening. The announcement stated that smartphones, computers, and other tech devices and components are exempted from his reciprocal tariffs. This includes exclusions for other electronic devices and components such as semiconductors, solar cells, flat panel TV displays, flash drives, and memory cards. According to the White House, these exemptions are intended to provide more time for US companies to move production back to the US. Nonetheless, there are some confusions over this as Trump made a U-turn again on Sunday claiming there will be no exemptions and electronics would be subjected to other tariffs. In our view, it is not easy to reshore the entire production capacity back to the US for various reasons. It will take years for the US to reshore production capacity due to its current weak infrastructure and labour shortages (both skilled and unskilled), compounded by the enforcement of new immigration rules to repatriate immigrants. The American Society of Civil Engineers' 2025 assessment gave the nation an overall grade of C for its infrastructure, the highest since it began grading in 1998. Out of the 18 categories assessed, 50% received either D or D+ grades, while the rest received various shades of Cs, except for Ports (B) and Rail (B-). Many critical categories like Aviation, Energy, Roads, Schools, and Transit received different shades of D due to decades of underinvestment. Therefore, significant local improvements are necessary before large-scale, productive reshoring can occur. Additionally, the US is currently near full employment, with industries such as healthcare, construction, and transportation particularly affected by demographic shifts, an aging workforce, and skills gaps. The tariffs will have a negative impact on Malaysia as exports rose 5.7% year-on-year to RM1.508 trillion in 2024. This growth was driven by increased shipments to ASEAN, the US, EU, and Taiwan, with exports to the US and Taiwan reaching all-time highs. The primary driver was the rise in shipments of electrical and electronic (E&E) products, which accounted for 39.9% of total exports. E&E exports increased by 4.5% to RM601.2 billion, marking the highest value ever recorded for the sector. Exports to China, Malaysia’s largest trading partner, dipped 2.2% to RM187.7 billion due to weaker demand for E&E products and metalliferous ores and metal scrap. Tariffs will further erode demand from China, which is the most affected country by the US reciprocal tariff of 145%. In contrast, exports to the US rose 23.2% to a record high of RM198.7 billion, driven by strong demand for E&E products, machinery, equipment and parts, and rubber products. Based on our initial estimates, a 24% tariff on Malaysia’s exports to the US would reduce Malaysia’s GDP by 1.2% points on a full-year basis. This figure assumes an export multiplier of 1.2x and a lower price elasticity of -0.8x due to inelastic demand caused by value-chain complexity, limited short-term substitution, long-term supplier relationships, and potential tariff absorption by firms. However, the full-year impact on 2025 will be mitigated by the 90- day pause and the temporary exemptions on certain export categories. Front-loading activities could increase during this 90-day period, contributing to a stronger-than-expected second quarter that may cushion the negative impact in the second half. Additionally, Malaysia may benefit from more orders and investments due to comparatively lower tariffs versus its developing peers. Therefore, our economics team prefers to maintain their 2025 GDP growth forecast between 4.8% and 5.3%, pending the outcome of discussions between the US and Malaysian governments at the end of this month or early May. Nonetheless, as the financial market typically reacts faster than the goods market, we are taking this opportunity to cut our 2025 FBMKLCI target to 1,660 (1,785 previously), based on a CY26 PER of 14.1x, which is a 3x discount from its last 5-year PER average of 17.1x. Our market strategy remains unchanged, focusing on three key themes: undervalued blue chips, domestic plays, and defensive stocks. Banks top the list of undervalued blue chips as proxies for Malaysia’s resilient economy. Construction and property companies are viewed as viable domestic plays, while the consumer, power & utilities, and telco sectors are regarded as relatively stable and less sensitive to economic cycles. We maintain our buy recommendations onMAYBANK, CIMB, PBBANK, TENAGA, TM, SIMEPROP, IOIPG, MAHSING, CMLT, KIPREIT, GAMUDA, SUNCON, BNASTRA, DPHARMA, F&N, FFB, and SAMAIDEN. (Bloomberg) BOE’s Greene says US dollar moves complicate tariff policy response Bank of England (BOE) policymaker Megan Greene said US tariffs will slow growth but the impact on inflation was less clear because the US dollar is not behaving as would be expected. Speaking at the Delphi Economic Forum in Greece on Saturday, Greene said the trade war unleashed by US President Donald Trump would be a “global demand shock” and the tariffs “will impact the economy.” But she added that “the implications for inflation are somewhat ambiguous.” Trump has caused wild swings in markets with his volatile policymaking, first raising tariffs across the world, then pausing them on most countries. By targeting China with 145% tariffs, you would normally see “export substitution and trade diversion that tends to be disinflationary” for other countries, Greene said.
